title: Parameter Identification
text:
Map simulation outputs to data

Mapping between simulated outputs and observed data is achieved through PIOutputMapping objects.
Model error calculation requires linking simulation outputs to observed data, where one output can be mapped to multiple data sets, summing errors from each pair.

title: Parameter Identification
text:
Configure parameter identification
Configure PI through a reusable PIConfiguration object:

piConfiguration <- PIConfiguration$new()

print(piConfiguration)

<PIConfiguration>
  • Optimization algorithm: BOBYQA
  • Confidence interval method: hessian
  • Objective function type: lsq
  • Residual weighting method: none
  • Robust residual calculation method: none
  • Print feedback after each function evaluation: FALSE


You can enable printing current parameter values and error at every objective evaluation by setting printEvaluationFeedback property to TRUE. Useful for diagnosing slow runs; noisy in normal use.







Note


The above configuration employs default configuration settings.

title: Parameter Identification
text:
Residual weighting
residualWeightingMethod decides how each residual is scaled before entering the cost sum:

"none" (default) — raw residuals; large-magnitude points dominate the fit.
"error" — divide residuals by yErrorValues of the observed dataset; points with smaller measurement error pull harder. Requires yErrorValues on data.

Implication: with "error" the optimizer trusts low-error points more, which typically tightens the fit at high-concentration peaks where reported errors are smaller in absolute terms and loosens it on noisy tail points.

title: Parameter Identification
section: Objective function type and robust estimation
text:
Objective function type and robust estimation
objectiveFunctionType selects the cost form:

"lsq" (default) — weighted least squares on uncensored points only.
"m3" — Beal’s M3: uncensored points use weighted SSR; censored (BLQ) points contribute their probability of being below LLOQ. Requires per-point LLOQ column on the dataset.

title: Parameter Identification
text:
Algorithm options
algorithm selects the optimizer; the choice drives convergence behaviour:

"BOBYQA" (default) — gradient-free local search; fast, but stuck at local optima if the start point is poor.
"HJKB" — Hooke–Jeeves with bounds; local, derivative-free, robust to non-smooth objectives.
"DEoptim" — differential evolution; global, parallel population search. Much slower, but escapes local minima.

title: Parameter Identification
text:
What is a confidence interval?
A point estimate (estimate column of piResults$toDataFrame()) is the single parameter value that minimizes the cost on this dataset. It says nothing about how certain that value is. A 95% confidence interval (CI) is a range that, under repeated sampling of new data from the same underlying process, would contain the true parameter value 95% of the time.
Equivalent intuition: the CI is the set of parameter values that are not statistically distinguishable from the optimum given the observed data and the assumed noise model. Outside the interval, the cost rises enough that the data favours the optimum over the trial value.

title: Parameter Identification
text:
Why estimate CIs?


Identifiability check — a CI that spans a large fraction of the prior range (or hits the bounds) means the data does not constrain that parameter; the point estimate is essentially arbitrary. Refit with more data, fix the parameter, or remove it from the optimization.
Decision-relevant uncertainty — downstream predictions (Cmax, AUC, dose recommendations) inherit the parameter uncertainty. Propagating CIs (or sampling from the covariance matrix) gives a credible range on the prediction, not a false-precision single number.
Model comparison — overlapping CIs between dose groups or studies suggest the parameter can be shared; non-overlapping CIs argue against pooling.
Reporting requirement — regulatory submissions and population-PK analyses expect parameter precision metrics (sd, cv, lowerCI, upperCI).

title: Parameter Identification
text:
Choosing ciMethod
ciMethod selects how CIs around the fitted parameters are computed:

"hessian" (default) — invert the numerical Hessian at the optimum; cheap, assumes locally quadratic cost surface, can be unreliable near bounds or for non-identifiable parameters.
"PL" (profile likelihood) — for each parameter, refit the others while sweeping its value to find the cost-rise threshold. More robust to non-linearity, but each parameter triggers many extra optimizations.
"bootstrap" — resample residuals/data, refit; non-parametric, no distributional assumption, but expensive (one full PI run per replicate).







Note


Profile likelihood (PL): starts from the optimum, fixes one parameter at a value slightly off the optimum, re-optimizes all others, records the resulting cost. Repeats over a sweep of the fixed parameter. The CI is the range of fixed values whose minimised cost stays within a chi-squared threshold of the global optimum. Captures parameter correlations and asymmetric cost surfaces that the Hessian linearisation misses.

title: Parameter Identification
text:
How to interpret the output

piResults$toDataFrame() after CI estimation populates:

estimate — point estimate.
sd — standard deviation derived from the CI method (Hessian → from inverse Fisher information; PL/bootstrap → from interval width).
cv — coefficient of variation (sd / |estimate|, %). Rough rule: CV < 30% well-determined, 30–50% moderately, > 50% poorly identified.
lowerCI / upperCI — interval bounds at the configured level (default 95%).
ciType — which method produced the bound (per-parameter, since PL can fall back to Hessian on failure).


Practical reads:

A CI hitting minValue or maxValue indicates the optimum is at a bound — the parameter wants to go further but is blocked. Either widen the bounds or accept the boundary as a hard constraint.
An asymmetric CI (e.g. lowerCI close to estimate but upperCI far above) signals a non-quadratic cost surface; trust PL or bootstrap over Hessian here.
NA bounds typically mean the CI method failed (e.g. Hessian singular near a flat direction). Switch to PL.

title: Parameter Identification
text:
Grid search
gridSearch() samples the joint parameter space: it builds a Latin-hypercube-style grid spanning the [minValue, maxValue] box of every PI parameter, runs each combination through the simulation + cost function, and returns one tibble row per evaluation with parameter values and OFV.

title: Parameter Identification
text:
Per-parameter OFV profiles
calculateOFVProfiles() sweeps one parameter at a time across a window around its current value, holding the others fixed. Returns a list of tibbles (one per parameter) with the swept value and the resulting OFV — the local cost curve for that parameter.
boundFactor (default 0.1) is multiplicative: window is currValue * (1 ± boundFactor). For lipophilicity ≈ -0.097 the default sweeps only ±0.0097 — vanishingly narrow. For parameters near zero, override boundFactor upwards or call the method only after a sensible non-zero currValue is in place.
What the curve tells you:


Sharp U around the optimum → the parameter is locally identifiable; the optimizer should land cleanly and the Hessian-based CI will be tight.
Flat profile → the data does not constrain this parameter in the swept window; optimization will wander, CIs will be wide, and the point estimate is essentially arbitrary. Fix the parameter, tighten bounds, or collect more informative data.
Asymmetric or kinked profile → cost surface non-quadratic; trust profile-likelihood CIs over Hessian.
Monotonic profile → the optimum is outside the swept window; widen boundFactor or revisit minValue/maxValue.



# Profile OFV for each parameter independently — list of tibbles, one per parameter
piTask$calculateOFVProfiles(totalEvaluations = 20)

title: Plot Generation
text: 🎯 Goal: Generate Plots using ESQlabsR

title: Plot Generation
text:
Plots view structure
All plots are defined in the Plots view of the ESQapp.
3 sub-sheets:







Sub-sheet
Purpose




DataCombined
Specify DataCombined objects (link simulation results with observed data, define group etc)


plotConfiguration
Define plot type, label texts, units, scales


plotGrids
Combine plots into multi-panel figures

title: Plot Generation
text:
ESQapp ↔︎ JSON Recap

The R workflow (createPlotsFromExcel() and friends) reads from the Excel files in Configurations/.
The ESQapp writes directly into the JSON snapshot (ProjectConfiguration.json).
After editing in the ESQapp, restore the Excel files before running the R workflow:

restoreProjectConfiguration("ProjectConfiguration.json")






Tip


Use projectConfigurationStatus() to check whether the Excel files and the JSON snapshot are in sync (covered in B2).

title: Plot Generation
text:
{esqlabsR} plot definition workflow





classDiagram
    direction LR
    class DataCombined {
        DataCombinedName
        dataType
        label
        scenario
        ...
    }
    class plotConfiguration {
        plotID
        DataCombinedName
        plotType
        title
        ...
    }
    class plotGrids {
        name
        plotID
        title
        ...
    }
    DataCombined --> plotConfiguration
    plotConfiguration --> plotGrids

    note for DataCombined "Simulation results and observed data"
    note for plotConfiguration "Data and type of each plot"
    note for plotGrids "Plots to combine on one panel"

title: Plot Generation
text:
Get the example project
{esqlabsR} ships with a ready-to-use TestProject. exampleProjectConfigurationPath() returns its ProjectConfiguration.xlsx.
The installed copy is read-only — copy it to a writable folder before editing in the ESQapp.


Code
trainingDir <- file.path(tempdir(), "esqlabsR_training")
dir.create(trainingDir, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)
file.copy(
  list.files(dirname(exampleProjectConfigurationPath()), full.names = TRUE),
  to = trainingDir,
  recursive = TRUE,
  overwrite = TRUE
)
projectConfigurationPath <- file.path(trainingDir, "ProjectConfiguration.xlsx")

title: Plot Generation
text:
Open in the ESQapp
Launch the ESQapp and open trainingDir. All exercises edit this copy.






Important


Save in the ESQapp (Export) after every edit. Then in R run restoreProjectConfiguration(file.path(trainingDir, "ProjectConfiguration.json")) before reading the configuration with {esqlabsR}.

title: Plot Generation
section: How to fill the DataCombined view
text:
How to fill the DataCombined view







Reminder


A DataCombined defines the data to be plotted. It can contain simulated results and/or observed data and links them together.

title: Plot Generation
section: How to fill the DataCombined view — steps
text:
How to fill the DataCombined view — steps

Give a name in the DataCombinedName column.
Set the label (mandatory) — used as data set name in legends and downstream operations.
Add the simulation results:

set dataType to simulated
set scenario to the scenario name (as defined in the Scenarios view)
set the path to the simulation output path of interest

Optional Add observed data:

set dataType to observed
set dataSet to the observed data set that will be imported.

Customise the DataCombined object:

set group, and more…

title: Plot Generation
text:
Grouping data sets
All rows of one DataCombined are drawn on the same plot.
group controls legend + visual styling:

Empty / unique group → each row gets distinct color, line/symbol style, separate legend entry (legend label = label).
Shared group across rows → rows share one color and collapse to a single legend entry (legend label = group).
Common pattern: pair a simulated row with its observed row under one group so they share color.

Reference: OSPSuite-R DataCombined grouping.







Inspect


After creating a DataCombined in R:
myDataCombined$AciclovirExercise$groupMap
myDataCombined$AciclovirExercise$toDataFrame()

title: Plot Generation
text:
Exercise 1: Create a new DataCombined
The TestProject already contains scenarios TestScenario (plasma output) and TestScenario2 (plasma + fat outputs).

In the ESQapp, open Plots > DataCombined.
Add 3 new rows: one named AciclovirExercise, two named PlasmaVsFat.

title: Plot Generation
text:
Load Observed Data
{esqlabsR} ships with:

Predefined observed data template (Data/*_TimeValuesData.xlsx).
The corresponding ImporterConfiguration (Data/esqlabs_dataImporter_configuration.xml).

Observed data is then loaded with loadObservedData().

title: Plot Generation
text:
Exercise 2: Add observed data

In the example project, open Data/TestProject_TimeValuesData.xlsx and identify the Aciclovir sheet.
Use loadObservedData() and assign the result to observedData.
Check the names of the observedData object.



Result
# 1: sheet is "Laskin 1982.Group A"

# 2
observedData <- loadObservedData(
  projectConfiguration = myProjectConfiguration,
  sheets = c("Laskin 1982.Group A")
)

# 3
names(observedData)

title: Plot Generation
text:
Create DataCombined from configuration
Once a DataCombined is defined in the ESQapp, it can be created in R with createDataCombinedFromExcel(). Three main arguments:

projectConfiguration the project configuration object (points at the Excel files).
dataCombinedNames the names of the DataCombined to create. If NULL all are created.
simulatedScenarios the simulated results to attach.

title: Plot Generation
text:
Exercise 3: Create DataCombined from configuration
Verify the DataCombined is correctly defined.

Create the DataCombined using createDataCombinedFromExcel().
Check its names.



Result
# 1
myDataCombined <- createDataCombinedFromExcel(
  projectConfiguration = myProjectConfiguration,
  dataCombinedNames = "AciclovirExercise",
  simulatedScenarios = results,
  observedData = observedData
)

# 2
names(myDataCombined)

title: Plot Generation
text:
Residual diagnostics
Quantify fit numerically with calculateResiduals() — operates on a single DataCombined and returns observed − simulated.

residuals <- calculateResiduals(
  dataCombined = myDataCombined$AciclovirExercise,
  scaling = "lin"
)

sum(residuals$residualValues)


scaling: "lin" or "log" (matches the y-axis of the corresponding plot).
Returns a data.frame with residualValues, xValues, and group info.







Tip


Visualise residuals via plotConfiguration plotType residualsVsTime or residualsVsSimulated.

title: Plot Generation
section: How to fill the plotConfiguration view
text:
How to fill the plotConfiguration view

Give an ID to the plot.
Reference the DataCombined object to use.
Select a plotType — one of individual, observedVsSimulated, residualsVsTime, residualsVsSimulated.
Optional Set plot options in the corresponding columns.









Tip


More options are available! Add a new column with the option name (use the “Add new column” action in the ESQapp).
Full list available in ospsuite::DefaultPlotConfiguration$new().











Cell values



Empty cell → default value.
Multi-value field → comma-separated (e.g. 0, 2 for limits).
Value containing a comma (e.g., title) → wrap in parentheses.

title: Plot Generation
text:
Exercise 4: Create new plots

In the ESQapp, open Plots > plotConfiguration.
Add 3 new rows. 2 referencing AciclovirExercise, 1 referencing PlasmaVsFat.
Set plotType to individual and observedVsSimulated for the AciclovirExercise rows. Set individual for PlasmaVsFat.
Customize: set a title. For PlasmaVsFatTime, set xValuesLimits to 0, 2 and xUnit to h.
Add supplementary settings (e.g. caption) of your choice.
Save in the ESQapp.








Naming plotID


plotID must be unique across the sheet. Pick descriptive names — they appear in error messages and are referenced from plotGrids. Suggested pattern: <DataCombined><PlotType>, e.g.:

AciclovirExerciseTime — AciclovirExercise + individual
AciclovirExerciseObsVsSim — AciclovirExercise + observedVsSimulated
PlasmaVsFatTime — PlasmaVsFat + individual

Avoid P1/P2/… — they collide with the seed plots already in the TestProject.






Solution

Your plotConfiguration sub-sheet should look like:

title: Plot Generation
section: How to fill the plotGrids view
text:
How to fill the plotGrids view
plotGrids are the objects used to draw the plots.
It must be used to generate any plot defined in plotConfiguration. It can combine several plots into a multi-panel figure.

title: Plot Generation
text:
Exercise 5: Create new plotGrids

In the ESQapp, open Plots > plotGrids. Add 2 rows: AciclovirExercise and PlasmaVsFat.
In AciclovirExercise, include the two AciclovirExercise plot IDs. In PlasmaVsFat, include the PlasmaVsFat plot ID.
Add a title.
Save in the ESQapp.



Solution

Your plotGrids sub-sheet should look like:

title: Plot Generation
text:
Exercise 6: Generate plots

Use createPlotsFromExcel() to generate AciclovirExercise and PlasmaVsFat.



Result
restoreProjectConfiguration(
  file.path(trainingDir, "ProjectConfiguration.json")
)

myPlots <- createPlotsFromExcel(
  plotGridNames = c("AciclovirExercise", "PlasmaVsFat"),
  simulatedScenarios = results,
  observedData = observedData,
  projectConfiguration = myProjectConfiguration
)

title: Plot Generation
text:
Reuse the B3 project
Plot the scenarios built in B3 — scenarioExercise (250 mg), scenarioDose100 (100 mg), scenarioNoRenalCl, Aciclovir_Female, plus the whole-blood output added in B3.






Important


Requires the project from B3. If skipped, re-run the B3 exercises first or open workshops/resources/B3/esqLabsR_training/.





Code
b3Dir <- here::here("workshops/resources/B3/esqLabsR_training")

restoreProjectConfiguration(file.path(b3Dir, "ProjectConfiguration.json"))

b3ProjectConfiguration <- createProjectConfiguration(
  file.path(b3Dir, "ProjectConfiguration.xlsx")
)

b3Scenarios <- createScenarios(
  readScenarioConfigurationFromExcel(projectConfiguration = b3ProjectConfiguration)
)
b3Results <- runScenarios(scenarios = b3Scenarios)

title: Plot Generation
text:
Exercise 7: Dose comparison
Compare plasma profiles for 250 mg vs 100 mg.

ESQapp → open b3Dir. Plots > DataCombined.
Add 2 rows named DoseCompare.
Row 1 — dataType simulated, scenario scenarioExercise, path Aciclovir_PVB, label 250 mg.
Row 2 — dataType simulated, scenario scenarioDose100, path Aciclovir_PVB, label 100 mg.
Leave group empty → distinct colors per dose.
plotConfiguration → row DoseCompareTime, DataCombinedName DoseCompare, plotType individual, title Aciclovir dose comparison, yAxisScale log.
plotGrids → row DoseCompare, plotID DoseCompareTime.
Save (Export).

title: Plot Generation
text:
Exercise 8: Renal clearance impact
Show how disabling tubular secretion changes the plasma profile (scenarioExercise vs scenarioNoRenalCl).

ESQapp → DataCombined. Add 2 rows named RenalImpact.
Row 1 — scenario scenarioExercise, path Aciclovir_PVB, label Default.
Row 2 — scenario scenarioNoRenalCl, path Aciclovir_PVB, label No renal clearance.
plotConfiguration → RenalImpactTime, plotType individual, title Renal clearance impact, yAxisScale log.
plotGrids → RenalImpact, plotID RenalImpactTime.
Save.



Result
b3Plots <- createPlotsFromExcel(
  plotGridNames = "RenalImpact",
  simulatedScenarios = b3Results,
  projectConfiguration = b3ProjectConfiguration
)

b3Plots$RenalImpact

title: Plot Generation
text:
Exercise 9: Plasma vs whole blood
Use the whole-blood output added in B3 to compare compartments for scenarioExercise.

ESQapp → DataCombined. Add 2 rows named CompartmentCompare.
Row 1 — scenario scenarioExercise, path Aciclovir_PVB, label Plasma.
Row 2 — scenario scenarioExercise, path , label Whole blood.
plotConfiguration → CompartmentCompareTime, plotType individual, title Plasma vs whole blood.
plotGrids → CompartmentCompare, plotID CompartmentCompareTime.
Save.







Note


B3 did not pin a name for the whole-blood output. Use the OutputPathId you chose (e.g. Aciclovir_WholeBlood).

title: Plot Generation
text:
Exercise 10: Combined summary
Pack Exercises 7–9 into one multi-panel figure.

ESQapp → plotGrids. Add row B3Summary, plotID DoseCompareTime, RenalImpactTime, CompartmentCompareTime, nrow 1, title B3 scenario summary.
Save.



Result
b3Plots <- createPlotsFromExcel(
  plotGridNames = "B3Summary",
  simulatedScenarios = b3Results,
  projectConfiguration = b3ProjectConfiguration
)

b3Plots$B3Summary

Project Structure

title: Project Initialization
text:
ProjectConfiguration.xlsx
Tells {esqlabsR} where to find each folder/file. Ships with sensible defaults; customize to match your project layout. This makes the workflow fully reproducible: anyone with the same files and configuration will get the same results.
Load the bundled example to inspect:

my_project_configuration <- createProjectConfiguration(
  path = esqlabsR::exampleProjectConfigurationPath(),
  ignoreVersionCheck = TRUE
)

title: PK Analysis
text:
Calculated from Simulation Results

PK parameters can be calculated for all outputs of a simulation.
Pass a SimulationResults object to calculatePKAnalyses().

pkAnalysis <- calculatePKAnalyses(results = simulationResults)

title: PK Analysis
text:
Get simulation results

# Load the package
library(ospsuite)

# Load the simulation
sim <- loadSimulation(system.file(
  "extdata",
  "Aciclovir.pkml",
  package = "ospsuite"
))

# Run the simulation
simulationResults <- runSimulations(sim)[[1]]

title: PK Analysis
text:
CSV Interoperability

PK parameters can be exported and imported through .csv files.
These files are interoperable between PK-Sim and {ospsuite}.

title: PK Analysis
text:
Import

# example file
PKAnalysisFilePath <- system.file(
  "extdata",
  "PKAnalyses.csv",
  package = "ospsuite"
)

# import to ospsuite framework
pkAnalysis <- importPKAnalysesFromCSV(
  filePath = PKAnalysisFilePath,
  simulation = sim
)







Warning


The related simulation must be loaded to import the PK parameters.

title: PK Analysis

Export

exportPKAnalysesToCSV(pkAnalyses = pkAnalysis, filePath = "PKAnalysis.csv")

Custom PK parameters

Useful when working on different time intervals or dimensions other than concentrations

Custom formulas are not supported

title: PK Analysis
text:
Exercise Overview
Practice PK parameter analysis on the Aciclovir simulation:

Plasma PK. Get C_max and C_tEnd for plasma concentrations.
Add whole blood output. Repeat for whole blood concentrations after adding the output.
Alternate dose. Build a 100 mg simulation and export its PK table.
Custom parameters. Define time-bounded C_max / C_min and compute ratio.

title: PK Analysis
text:
Baseline PK
Let’s practice! Start by accessing and saving into an object the value of C_max and C_tEnd for the Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood).


Solution
library(ospsuite)

# Simulation Setup
simExercise <- loadSimulation(
  system.file("extdata", "Aciclovir.pkml", package = "ospsuite"),
  loadFromCache = FALSE
)
simExercise_res <- runSimulations(simExercise)[[1]]

# We create the PK analysis
pkAnalysisExercise <- calculatePKAnalyses(results = simExercise_res)

# And access our PK parameters of interest
C_maxExercise <- pkAnalysisExercise$pKParameterFor(
  quantityPath = "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)",
  pkParameter = "C_max"
)$values

C_tEndExercise <- pkAnalysisExercise$pKParameterFor(
  quantityPath = "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)",
  pkParameter = "C_tEnd"
)$values

title: PK Analysis
text:
Whole Blood Concentration
Repeat the process but with the following output: "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Whole Blood (Peripheral Venous Blood)"


Solution
# We do the same for another output of our choice, here the whole blood
# First, we need to add the output to the simulation, and re run it
addOutputs(
  "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Whole Blood (Peripheral Venous Blood)",
  simulation = simExercise
)
simExercise_res <- runSimulations(simExercise)[[1]]

pkAnalysisExercise <- calculatePKAnalyses(results = simExercise_res)

C_maxExercise_wholeBlood <- pkAnalysisExercise$pKParameterFor(
  quantityPath = "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Whole Blood (Peripheral Venous Blood)",
  pkParameter = "C_max"
)$values

C_tendExercise_wholeBlood <- pkAnalysisExercise$pKParameterFor(
  quantityPath = "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Whole Blood (Peripheral Venous Blood)",
  pkParameter = "C_tEnd"
)$values

title: PK Analysis
text:
100 mg Simulation
Create a new simulation with a dose of 100 mg, run it, then create a data frame called pkAnalysisDF_100mg containing all PK parameters of the Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood). Export the PK analysis to a csv file.


Reminder
# Simulation Setup
simFilePath <- system.file("extdata", "Aciclovir.pkml", package = "ospsuite")

simDose100 <- loadSimulation(simFilePath, loadFromCache = FALSE)

Dose100 <- getParameter(
  "Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Dose",
  simDose100
)
setParameterValues(Dose100, 100, units = "mg")

# We run the simulation
simDose100_res <- runSimulations(simDose100)[[1]]

title: PK Analysis
text:
100 mg PK Data Frame + Export


Solution
pkAnalysis_100mg <- calculatePKAnalyses(results = simDose100_res)

# First, let's create the data frame and have a look at it
pkAnalysisDF_100mg <- pkAnalysesToDataFrame(pkAnalysis_100mg)
pkAnalysisDF_100mg



  



Solution
# Then, we export the PK parameters
# Note: we can do both things independently
exportPKAnalysesToCSV(
  pkAnalyses = pkAnalysis_100mg,
  filePath = "pkAnalysis_100mg.csv"
)

title: PK Analysis
text:
Custom C_max and C_min
Now, add two PK parameters, C_max and C_min, between 10 and 90 minutes, for the plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood). Then, compute the ratio C_max/C_min for the original simulation and for the new one with a dose of 100 mg.


Solution
myC_max <- addUserDefinedPKParameter(
  name = "myC_max",
  standardPKParameter = StandardPKParameter$C_max
)

myC_min <- addUserDefinedPKParameter(
  name = "myC_min",
  standardPKParameter = StandardPKParameter$C_min
)

myC_max$startTime <- 10
myC_max$endTime <- 90

myC_min$startTime <- 10
myC_min$endTime <- 90

pkAnalysisExercise <- calculatePKAnalyses(results = simExercise_res)
pkAnalysis_100mg <- calculatePKAnalyses(results = simDose100_res)

title: PK Analysis
text:
Ratio — Original Simulation


Solution
# We compute the PK parameters for the original simulation
pk_cmax <- pkAnalysisExercise$pKParameterFor(
  quantityPath = "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)",
  pkParameter = "myC_max"
)

pk_cmin <- pkAnalysisExercise$pKParameterFor(
  quantityPath = "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)",
  pkParameter = "myC_min"
)

ratioExercise <- pk_cmax$values / pk_cmin$values

ratioExercise


[1] 4.615281

title: PK Analysis
text:
Ratio — 100 mg Simulation


Solution
# And for the new one
pk_cmax_100mg <- pkAnalysis_100mg$pKParameterFor(
  quantityPath = "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)",
  pkParameter = "myC_max"
)
pk_cmin_100mg <- pkAnalysis_100mg$pKParameterFor(
  quantityPath = "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)",
  pkParameter = "myC_min"
)

# The same ratio is obtained, but C_max and C_min values are different
ratio100mg <- pk_cmax_100mg$values / pk_cmin_100mg$values

ratio100mg


[1] 4.615281

title: Dimensions & Units
text:
In PK-Sim and MoBi
PK-Sim uses the concept of Dimensions and Units

A dimension represents the type of quantity (e.g. Volume, Mass, Concentration)
A unit is one of several ways to measure a dimension (e.g. L, mL; kg, g; mg/L, µg/mL)


The software uses a base unit for each dimension

Volume is in L
Mass is in kg
Concentration (molar) is in µmol/L

title: Dimensions & Units
text:
Base vs Display Units






Note


Although users can set or visualize a quantity in different units, OSPS always uses the base unit for calculations.










Important


The base units are often different from the units displayed by default in PK-Sim. Those are called the display units.





Full List of Dimensions and Units

title: Dimensions & Units
text:
Base Units Internally
Like in PK-Sim and MoBi, {ospsuite} uses base units internally. Users interact with the package through display units or any other supported unit.

library(ospsuite)

sim <- loadSimulation(system.file(
  "extdata",
  "Aciclovir.pkml",
  package = "ospsuite"
))

bmiParam <- getParameter("Organism|BMI", sim)

bmiParam



<Parameter>
  • Quantity Type: Parameter
  • Path: Organism|BMI
  • Value: 0.23 [kg/dm²]
  • Value Origin: Other-Standard definition

── Formula ──

  • isExplicit: TRUE
  • formula: Height>0 ? BW / (Height)^2 : 0
  • Value overrides formula: FALSE

title: Dimensions & Units
text:
Unit Fields on a Parameter







Base and Display units are listed in Parameters



bmiParam$unit

[1] "kg/dm²"

bmiParam$displayUnit

[1] "kg/m²"

bmiParam$allUnits

[1] "kg/m²"  "kg/dm²"






kg/dm² is the base unit for BMI. Display unit is kg/m².

title: Dimensions & Units
text:
{ospsuite} Unit Conversion Functions
The package provides a set of functions for easy conversion between units of a same dimension.


toUnit() to convert from one unit to another
toBaseUnit() to convert from any unit to the base unit
toDisplayUnit() to convert from the base unit to the display unit




ospsuite::toUnit(
  quantityOrDimension = "Volume",
  values = 1,
  sourceUnit = "L",
  targetUnit = "mL"
)

[1] 1000





ospsuite::toBaseUnit(quantityOrDimension = "Volume", values = 1000, unit = "mL")

[1] 1

title: Dimensions & Units
text:
Convert to Display Unit

bmiParam

<Parameter>
  • Quantity Type: Parameter
  • Path: Organism|BMI
  • Value: 0.23 [kg/dm²]
  • Value Origin: Other-Standard definition

── Formula ──

  • isExplicit: TRUE
  • formula: Height>0 ? BW / (Height)^2 : 0
  • Value overrides formula: FALSE




toDisplayUnit(quantity = bmiParam, values = 0.23)

[1] 23

title: Dimensions & Units
text:
Additional Arguments
In some situations, the molWeight and molWeightUnit arguments are required to convert between Weight and Molar Concentrations.


ospsuite::toUnit(
  quantityOrDimension = "Concentration (mass)",
  values = 100,
  sourceUnit = "mg/L",
  targetUnit = "mol/L",
  molWeight = 225.21,
  molWeightUnit = "g/mol"
)

[1] 0.00044403

title: Dimensions & Units
text:
Set Parameter Values
The function setParameterValues() can be used to set the value of a parameter from a specific unit.

setParameterValues(bmiParam, 25)

bmiParam

<Parameter>
  • Quantity Type: Parameter
  • Path: Organism|BMI
  • Value: 25.00 [kg/dm²]
  • Value Origin: Other-Standard definition

── Formula ──

  • isExplicit: TRUE

title: Dimensions & Units
text:
DataCombined
A DataCombined may hold simulation results and observed data in different units. Use convertUnits() to align them, or to change units for reporting.

sim_res <- runSimulations(sim)[[1]]
filePath <- system.file(
  "extdata",
  "ObsDataAciclovir_1.pkml",
  package = "ospsuite"
)
dataSet <- loadDataSetFromPKML(filePath = filePath)

myDataCombined <- DataCombined$new()
myDataCombined$addSimulationResults(
  simulationResults = sim_res
)
myDataCombined$addDataSets(
  dataSet,
  names = "Observed (Vergin 1995.Iv)",
  groups = "Aciclovir"
)

title: Dimensions & Units
text:
Inspect Original Units

myDataCombined$toDataFrame()


  



Original data has Time in minutes and Concentration in µmol/mL.

title: Dimensions & Units
text:
convertUnits()
The function convertUnits() can be used to convert the units of the DataCombined.

convertUnits(myDataCombined, xUnit = "h", yUnit = "mg/L")

title: Dimensions & Units
text:
Exercise Overview
Practice unit handling on the Aciclovir Dose parameter:

Access the Dose parameter. Use getParameter().
Inspect units. Base unit and display unit.
Convert kg → mg. Try toUnit() and toDisplayUnit().
Change value. Set Dose to 100 mg with setParameterValues().
Verify. Check if the stored unit changed.

title: Dimensions & Units
text:
Access the Dose Parameter
Access the administration Dose from the simulation. What is the base unit for this parameter?

sim <- loadSimulation(system.file(
  "extdata",
  "Aciclovir.pkml",
  package = "ospsuite"
))



Solution
Dose <- getParameter(
  "Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Dose",
  sim
)

Dose$unit


[1] "kg"



What is the display unit of the Dose?


Solution
Dose$displayUnit


[1] "mg"



The base unit is kg and the display unit is mg.

title: Dimensions & Units
text:
Convert kg to mg
By using two functions of your choice among toUnit(), toBaseUnit() and toDisplayUnit(), convert the Dose value from kg to mg.


Solution
# We can use toUnit and toDisplayUnit
ospsuite::toUnit(
  quantityOrDimension = Dose,
  values = Dose$value,
  sourceUnit = "kg",
  targetUnit = "mg"
)


[1] 250





Solution
ospsuite::toDisplayUnit(quantity = Dose, values = Dose$value)


[1] 250




Then, use setParameterValues() to change the value to 100 mg.


Solution
setParameterValues(Dose, 100, units = "mg")

title: Dimensions & Units
text:
Verify Stored Unit
Display the Dose object. Has the unit changed?


Solution
Dose


<Parameter>
  • Quantity Type: Parameter
  • Path: Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Dose
  • Value: 1.00e-04 [kg]

── Formula ──

  • isConstant: TRUE



The unit is still in kg, as the base unit remains the same.

title: Run Simulations
section: 🎯 Goal: Learn how to run simulations using {ospsuite}
text: 🎯 Goal: Learn how to run simulations using {ospsuite}

title: Run Simulations
text:
runSimulations()
Use runSimulations() for every run — one simulation or many.

Takes a list of Simulation objects, or a single Simulation object.
Returns a named list of SimulationResults objects, keyed by simulation id.

runSimulations(sim)         # single
runSimulations(list(sim1, sim2))  # multiple







Deprecated


runSimulation() (singular) is deprecated. Do not use it in new code.

title: Run Simulations
text:
Run a Single Simulation

library(ospsuite)

sim <- loadSimulation(system.file(
  "extdata",
  "Aciclovir.pkml",
  package = "ospsuite"
))

simulationResults <- runSimulations(sim)

# runSimulations() always returns a list — unwrap to get the SimulationResults
simulationResult <- simulationResults[[1]]
simulationResult

<SimulationResults>
  • Number of individuals: 1
For paths:
  • Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)
  • Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|Aciclovir|Plasma Unbound

title: Run Simulations
text:
Run Multiple Simulations

sim1 <- loadSimulation(system.file(
  "extdata",
  "Aciclovir.pkml",
  package = "ospsuite"
))
sim2 <- loadSimulation(system.file(
  "extdata",
  "Aciclovir.pkml",
  package = "ospsuite"
))

simulationResults <- runSimulations(simulations = list(sim1, sim2))

simulationResults

$`vDD7ZmLwP0-KF-dvMbwaMQ`
<SimulationResults>
  • Number of individuals: 1
For paths:
  • Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)
  • Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|Aciclovir|Plasma Unbound

$Ow9ca1biEUeidRMkZ1JXAQ
<SimulationResults>
  • Number of individuals: 1
For paths:
  • Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)
  • Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|Aciclovir|Plasma Unbound

title: Run Simulations
text:
Result List Names = Simulation IDs
The names of the list returned by runSimulations() are the simulation ids (sim$id), not the R variable names. Use them to look up a specific result.

names(simulationResults)

[1] "vDD7ZmLwP0-KF-dvMbwaMQ" "Ow9ca1biEUeidRMkZ1JXAQ"




# Each simulation carries its own id
c(sim1$id, sim2$id)

[1] "vDD7ZmLwP0-KF-dvMbwaMQ" "Ow9ca1biEUeidRMkZ1JXAQ"





# Access a result by id
simulationResults[[sim1$id]]

<SimulationResults>
  • Number of individuals: 1
For paths:
  • Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)
  • Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|Aciclovir|Plasma Unbound

title: Run Simulations
text:
Access simulation results values
A SimulationResults object does not expose values directly. Extract them with one of the helpers below.
First, pick a single SimulationResults out of the list returned by runSimulations().

simulationResult <- simulationResults[[1]]

title: Run Simulations
text:
getOutputValues()

outputValues <- getOutputValues(simulationResult)
names(outputValues)

[1] "data"     "metaData"

title: Run Simulations
text:
Output Values — data
Contains a basic data.frame with simulation results values.

outputValues$data

title: Run Simulations
text:
Output Values — metaData
Contains context for the simulation results values.

outputValues$metaData

$`Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)`
$`Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)`$unit
[1] "µmol/l"

$`Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)`$dimension
[1] "Concentration (molar)"


$`Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|Aciclovir|Plasma Unbound`
$`Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|Aciclovir|Plasma Unbound`$unit
[1] "µmol/l"

$`Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|Aciclovir|Plasma Unbound`$dimension
[1] "Concentration (molar)"


$Time
$Time$unit
[1] "min"

$Time$dimension
[1] "Time"

title: Run Simulations
text:
Pick Specific Outputs
getOutputValues() returns all outputs by default. Pass quantitiesOrPaths to restrict to one or more paths.

plasmaPath <- "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)"
getOutputValues(simulationResult, quantitiesOrPaths = plasmaPath)$data

title: Run Simulations
text:
simulationResultsToDataFrame()
Gathers simulation results values and metadata into a single data.frame.

head(
  simulationResultsToDataFrame(simulationResult)
)

title: Run Simulations
text:
SimulationRunOptions
runSimulations() accepts a simulationRunOptions argument built with SimulationRunOptions$new(). Use it to tune parallelism, control negative value checks, and more.

options <- SimulationRunOptions$new(
  numberOfCores = 4, # run sims in parallel
  showProgress = TRUE, # print progress bar
  checkForNegativeValues = TRUE
)

simulationResults <- runSimulations(
  simulations = list(sim1, sim2),
  simulationRunOptions = options
)

Parallel execution matters when the list holds many simulations or running population simulations — scaling is near-linear up to physical core count.

title: Run Simulations
text:
Handling Failed Runs
If a simulation fails, its slot in the returned list is NULL. Always check before extracting values.

results <- runSimulations(list(sim1, sim2))

# Keep only successful runs
successful <- Filter(Negate(is.null), results)

# Or inspect which failed
failed_ids <- names(results)[vapply(results, is.null, logical(1))]

title: Run Simulations
text:
Export to CSV
exportResultsToCSV() writes a SimulationResults object to disk. Useful for caching long-running simulations or sharing with colleagues.

exportResultsToCSV(simulationResult, "aciclovir_results.csv")

title: Run Simulations
text:
Import from CSV
importResultsFromCSV() reconstructs a SimulationResults object from a previously exported CSV. Requires the original Simulation object.

simulationResult <- importResultsFromCSV(sim, "aciclovir_results.csv")

title: Run Simulations
text:
Exercise Overview
Compare two doses of Aciclovir end-to-end:

Load & parameterize. Two simulations, one with the default dose and one at 100 mg.
Run both. A single call to runSimulations().
Extract results. Pull the output values out of the returned list.
Compare. Maximum plasma concentration for each run.

title: Run Simulations
text:
Load and create two simulations

Load the example Aciclovir model to create two simulations: sim and sim_100mg
Set Dose parameter of sim_100mg to 100 mg (path: Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Dose)
Run both simulations



Answer 1 & 2
library(ospsuite)

# 1)
simFilePath <- system.file("extdata", "Aciclovir.pkml", package = "ospsuite")

sim <- loadSimulation(simFilePath)
sim_100mg <- loadSimulation(simFilePath)

# 2)
setParameterValuesByPath(
  parameterPaths = "Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Dose",
  values = 100,
  units = "mg",
  simulation = sim_100mg
)

title: Run Simulations
text:
Run Both Simulations


Answer 3
# 3)
simulationResults <- runSimulations(list(sim, sim_100mg))

title: Run Simulations
text:
Extract the simulation results

Extract the output values of sim and sim_100mg and assign them to results_sim and results_sim_100mg respectively.
Get the maximum concentration of Aciclovir in the Plasma for both simulations.



Answer
# 1)
names(simulationResults)

results_sim <- simulationResultsToDataFrame(simulationResults[[sim$id]])
results_sim_100mg <- simulationResultsToDataFrame(simulationResults[[
  sim_100mg$id
]])

# 2) Aciclovir plasma is the only output in the default pkml,
#    so max() over simulationValues is the plasma Cmax.
max(results_sim$simulationValues)
max(results_sim_100mg$simulationValues)

#> 50.25272
#> 20.10109

section: 🎯 Goal: Generate a virtual population and run a simulation across all individuals
text: 🎯 Goal: Generate a virtual population and run a simulation across all individuals

title: Population Simulations
text:
Overview

Define population characteristics
Generate the population with createPopulation()
Export/import populations via CSV
Run a population simulation
Retrieve and plot results

title: Population Simulations
text:
Load Sample Simulation

library(ospsuite)

simFilePath <- system.file(
  "extdata",
  "Aciclovir.pkml",
  package = "ospsuite"
)
sim <- loadSimulation(simFilePath)

title: Population Simulations
text:
createPopulationCharacteristics()
Describes the population before sampling.
Key arguments:

species, population
numberOfIndividuals
proportionOfFemales (percent, 0–100)
weightMin / weightMax (weightUnit, default "kg")
heightMin / heightMax (heightUnit, default "m")
ageMin / ageMax (ageUnit, default "year(s)")
moleculeOntogenies

title: Population Simulations
text:
Example

popCharacteristics <- createPopulationCharacteristics(
  species = Species$Human,
  population = HumanPopulation$Asian_Tanaka_1996,
  numberOfIndividuals = 50,
  proportionOfFemales = 50,
  weightMin = 30,
  weightMax = 98,
  weightUnit = "kg",
  ageMin = 0,
  ageMax = 80,
  ageUnit = "year(s)"
)

popCharacteristics

<PopulationCharacteristics>
  • Species: Human
  • Population: Asian_Tanaka_1996
  • Number of individuals: 50
  • Proportion of females: 50
  • Age: [0.00 year(s)..80.00 year(s)]
  • Gestational age: ]-Inf..+Inf[
  • Weight: [30.00 kg..98.00 kg]
  • Height: ]-Inf..+Inf[
  • BMI: ]-Inf..+Inf[


The population argument selects a built-in physiological database from PK-Sim. Each entry in HumanPopulation corresponds to a published reference dataset (e.g., Asian_Tanaka_1996 is based on Tanaka et al. 1996). Run names(HumanPopulation) to see all available options. # Generate Population

title: Population Simulations
text:
createPopulation()
Samples all individuals at once.

result <- createPopulation(
  populationCharacteristics = popCharacteristics
)

myPopulation <- result$population
myPopulation

<Population>
  • Number of Individuals: 50

popDf <- populationToDataFrame(myPopulation)
dim(popDf)

Export

populationToCSV(
  population = myPopulation,
  filePath = "myPopulation.csv"
)

Import

loadedPop <- loadPopulation(csvPopulationFile = "myPopulation.csv")

Use CSV to share populations across machines or reuse them without regenerating via PK-Sim.

simRunOptions <- SimulationRunOptions$new()
simRunOptions$numberOfCores <- 3

text:
Exercise Overview

Create a 20-individual European population, ages 25–65, weights 55–95 kg
Run the Aciclovir simulation for that population
Plot plasma profiles of individuals 1 and 2

Build Population and Run


Solution
popChar <- createPopulationCharacteristics(
  species = Species$Human,
  population = HumanPopulation$European_ICRP_2002,
  numberOfIndividuals = 20,
  proportionOfFemales = 50,
  weightMin = 55,
  weightMax = 95,

sim <- loadSimulation(simFilePath)
popResults <- runSimulations(simulations = sim, population = pop)[[1]]

Plot Two Individuals

dc$addSimulationResults(

section: 🎯 Goal: Understand and Handle Table Parameters

title: Table Parameters
text:
Concept

Table parameters in OSPS
Parameters can be defined as Tables made up out of pairs of x and y values.
The parameter value will be linearly interpolated between the specified values.




Examples of parameters defined by a table:

Enzyme ontogeny
Compound solubility

title: Table Parameters
text:
Load an Example

library(ospsuite)
# Load a simulation
sim <- loadSimulation(system.file(
  "extdata",
  "simple.pkml",
  package = "ospsuite"
))

# Get the parameter defined by a table.
tableParam <- getParameter("Organism|TableParameter", sim)

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
R concepts in 60 seconds

Variable — named container for a value; created with x <- 5 (read “x gets 5”).
Function — reusable operation; called with name(args), returns a value.
Argument — input to a function; passed by position mean(c(1,2)) or by name mean(x = c(1,2), na.rm = TRUE).
Package — bundle of functions + data; loaded with library(pkg), called as pkg::fn().

Tidyverse highlights

dplyr::filter(df, x > 0) — keep rows where the condition is true.
dplyr::mutate(df, new = old * 2) — add or overwrite columns.
dplyr::select(df, a, b) — pick columns by name.
dplyr::summarise(df, m = mean(x)) — collapse rows into one summary row (use with group_by).
ggplot2::ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) + geom_point() — build a plot layer by layer.

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
{ospsuite} — what it does

Loads .pkml simulation files into memory
Exposes parameters, molecules, compartments as R objects
Runs simulations (single or batch, parallel)
Returns time–concentration (or other model outputs) arrays
Computes PK parameters and sensitivity indices
Plots (ggplot2-based)

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
R6 reference semantics
{ospsuite} uses R6 classes. Reference semantics mean:
sim2 <- sim         # NOT a copy — same object
setParameterValuesByPath("Liver|Volume", 2, sim)
sim2  # also changed!

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Working With R6 Objects
Access fields/methods with $; construct with ClassName$new():
sim$solver
sim$outputSelections

dc <- DataCombined$new()
dc$addSimulationResults(
  simulationResults = simulationResults,
  quantitiesOrPaths = outputPath
)
Practical rules:

setParameterValues(), addOutputs(), clearOutputs(), addOutputInterval() mutate their argument — no reassignment
reset() on a parameter restores its pkml default without reloading
Fresh independent state: call loadSimulation(path) again

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
The 7-step {ospsuite} workflow

Load simulation
Inspect parameters / molecules / outputs
Modify parameters
Run simulation
Extract results
Analyze (PK, SA)
Plot


Today we walk all 7 steps with an example of Aciclovir PBPK model.

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Loading a simulation

library(ospsuite)

# bundled with the package
sim_path <- system.file("extdata", "Aciclovir.pkml", package = "ospsuite")
sim <- loadSimulation(sim_path)

print(sim)

<Simulation>

  • Source file: D:/GitHub
  repositories/ESQlabs/PAGE2026-R-WORKSHOP/renv/library/windows/R-4.6/x86_64-w64-mingw32/ospsuite/extdata/Aciclovir.pkml

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Inspect the simulation
Once loaded, the sim object exposes its configuration as fields:

sim$solver # solver tolerances + step bounds

<SolverSettings>
  • useJacobian: TRUE
  • h0: 1e-10
  • hMin: 0
  • hMax: 60
  • mxStep: 100000
  • relTol: 1e-05
  • absTol: 1e-10

sim$outputSelections # which quantities are recorded

<OutputSelections>
  • Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood):
  Drug, Observer
  • Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|Aciclovir|Plasma Unbound: Drug, Observer

sim$outputSchema # simulation time + output intervals

<OutputSchema>

── Output intervals ──

<Interval>
  • Name: Simulation Interval 1
  • Start time: 0.00e+00 [min]
  • End time: 15.00 [min]
  • Resolution: 1.00 [pts/min]
<Interval>
  • Name: Simulation Interval 2
  • Start time: 15.00 [min]
  • End time: 1440.00 [min]
  • Resolution: 0.33 [pts/min]
<Interval>
  • Name: Simulation Interval 3
  • Start time: 120.00 [min]
  • End time: 1440.00 [min]
  • Resolution: 0.07 [pts/min]



Walk through each field on screen — point out absTol, relTol, mxStep in solver; show which compartments will be in the result; show start/end time + resolution.

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Parameter paths — the addressing system
Every parameter, molecule, container in the simulation is addressed by a path:
Organism | Liver | Interstitial | Volume
^         ^       ^              ^
container container container    parameter

Hierarchical, |-delimited
Each layer = a container; last element = parameter / molecule amount / observer
Same syntax for molecules: Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|Aciclovir

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Simulation tree — explore interactively
getSimulationTree(sim) returns a navigable list tree of every path in the model. Best way to discover what’s in a model.

tree <- getSimulationTree(sim)

# tab-complete from RStudio: tree$Organism$Liver$Interstitial$Volume
tree$Organism$PeripheralVenousBlood$Aciclovir$`Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)`

$path
[1] "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)"



Demo live in RStudio: tab-complete from tree$ and watch the model unfold.

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Get one parameter — getParameter()

p <- getParameter("Organism|Liver|Interstitial|Volume", sim)
p$value # numeric value in base unit

[1] 0.3533005

p$unit # display unit (e.g. "L")

[1] "l"

p$dimension # "Volume"

[1] "Volume"



Short paths work too if you pass a sub-container as the second arg:

liver <- getContainer("Organism|Liver", sim)
getParameter("Interstitial|Volume", liver)

<Parameter>
  • Quantity Type: Parameter
  • Path: Organism|Liver|Interstitial|Volume
  • Value: 0.35 [l]
  • Value Origin: Publication-Willmann S, Lippert J, Sevestre M, Solodenko J,
  Fois F, Schmitt W. PK-Sim®: a physiologically based pharmacokinetic
  ‘whole-body’ model. Biosilico. 2003; 1 (4): 121-124.
  http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/S1478-5382%2803%2902342-4

── Formula ──

  • isExplicit: TRUE
  • formula: f_int*V
  • Value overrides formula: FALSE

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Find paths by pattern — getAllParametersMatching()
Wildcards: * = exactly one path element, ** = any number of elements.

# all parameters named "Volume" anywhere in the model
head(getAllParametersMatching("**|Volume", sim), 3)

[[1]]
<Parameter>
  • Quantity Type: Parameter
  • Path: Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|Volume
  • Value: 1.00 [l]

── Formula ──

  • isConstant: TRUE

[[2]]
<Parameter>
  • Quantity Type: Parameter
  • Path: Organism|VenousBlood|Volume
  • Value: 0.91 [l]
  • Value Origin: Publication-ICRP, 2002. Basic Anatomical and Physiological
  Data for Use in Radiological Protection Reference Values. ICRP Publication
  89. Ann. ICRP 32 (3-4). https://doi.org/10.1016/0146-6453(79)90123-4

── Formula ──

  • isDistributed: TRUE

[[3]]
<Parameter>
  • Quantity Type: Parameter
  • Path: Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|Volume
  • Value: 0.50 [l]
  • Value Origin: Publication-Willmann S, Lippert J, Sevestre M, Solodenko J,
  Fois F, Schmitt W. PK-Sim®: a physiologically based pharmacokinetic
  ‘whole-body’ model. Biosilico. 2003; 1 (4): 121-124.
  http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/S1478-5382%2803%2902342-4

── Formula ──

  • isExplicit: TRUE
  • formula: (1-HCT)*V
  • Value overrides formula: FALSE

# all "Volume" parameters in compartments named "Interstitial"
# with exactly one container between Organism and Interstitial
head(getAllContainersMatching("Organism|*|Interstitial", sim), 3)

[[1]]
<Container>
  • Container type: Compartment
  • Path: Organism|Bone|Interstitial

[[2]]
<Container>
  • Container type: Compartment
  • Path: Organism|Brain|Interstitial

[[3]]
<Container>
  • Container type: Compartment
  • Path: Organism|Fat|Interstitial

# all parameter paths under Liver (no wildcards needed)
getAllParameterPathsIn(liver)[1:5]

[1] "Organism|Liver|Fraction endosomal"           
[2] "Organism|Liver|Weight (tissue)"              
[3] "Organism|Liver|Fluid recirculation flow rate"
[4] "Organism|Liver|Lymph flow rate"              
[5] "Organism|Liver|Volume (endothelium)"         



Demo each on screen. Bridge to hands-on: attendees explore Liver + find a Volume parameter.

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
🖐️ Hands-on 1 — explore the model (15 min)
Open exercises/PAGE_2026/block1.R in RStudio. Tasks:

Load the bundled Aciclovir.pkml
Build the simulation tree with getSimulationTree(sim)
Tab-complete from tree$Organism$... — find the Liver volume parameter
Use getParameter() to read its value, unit, and dimension
Use getAllParametersMatching("**|Volume", sim) to list every Volume in the model


⏰ 15 min · Wilbert is in the room — flag him over for blockers


Solutions in exercises/PAGE_2026/solutions/block1.R. Common gotchas: forgetting system.file, escaping | in regex (don’t — these are not regex), confusing * and ** wildcards.

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Goal of Block 2

Modify parameters · run · compare simulation vs observed data.

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
What can be changed
Once a .pkml model is loaded in {ospsuite}, you can change from R:

Parameters (accessed by path)
Output paths (which quantities are recorded)
Simulation time + output intervals
Solver settings (tolerances, step bounds)



We’ll touch all four. Parameters first.

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Set a parameter value

ageParam <- getParameter("Organism|Age", sim)
ageParam

<Parameter>
  • Quantity Type: Parameter
  • Path: Organism|Age
  • Value: 25.70 [year(s)]

── Formula ──

  • isConstant: TRUE




setParameterValues(ageParam, 55, units = "year(s)")
ageParam

<Parameter>
  • Quantity Type: Parameter
  • Path: Organism|Age
  • Value: 55.00 [year(s)]

── Formula ──

  • isConstant: TRUE








Warning


Always pass units. Base units (kg, L, mol) often differ from display units (g, mL, µmol).

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Set values by path — shortcut
setParameterValuesByPath() combines getParameter() + setParameterValues() in one call. Accepts a single path or a vector.

setParameterValuesByPath(
  parameterPaths = c("Organism|Age", "Organism|Weight"),
  values = c(40, 75),
  units = c("year(s)", "kg"),
  simulation = sim
)

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Reset parameter values
Restore the .pkml default with $reset() — no need to reload the simulation.

ageParam$reset()
ageParam

<Parameter>
  • Quantity Type: Parameter
  • Path: Organism|Age
  • Value: 25.70 [year(s)]

── Formula ──

  • isConstant: TRUE

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Change simulation outputs
Defaults come from the imported .pkml:

sim$outputSelections

<OutputSelections>
  • Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood):
  Drug, Observer
  • Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|Aciclovir|Plasma Unbound: Drug, Observer



Add another output with addOutputs():

addOutputs(
  "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Whole Blood (Peripheral Venous Blood)",
  simulation = sim
)

sim$outputSelections

<OutputSelections>
  • Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood):
  Drug, Observer
  • Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|Aciclovir|Plasma Unbound: Drug, Observer
  • Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Whole Blood (Peripheral Venous
  Blood): Drug, Observer

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Change simulation time
Default simulation time comes from the .pkml:

sim$outputSchema

<OutputSchema>

── Output intervals ──

<Interval>
  • Name: Simulation Interval 1
  • Start time: 0.00e+00 [min]
  • End time: 15.00 [min]
  • Resolution: 1.00 [pts/min]
<Interval>
  • Name: Simulation Interval 2
  • Start time: 15.00 [min]
  • End time: 1440.00 [min]
  • Resolution: 0.33 [pts/min]
<Interval>
  • Name: Simulation Interval 3
  • Start time: 120.00 [min]
  • End time: 1440.00 [min]
  • Resolution: 0.07 [pts/min]

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Add an output interval
addOutputInterval() adds a new interval to the output schema (in minutes).

addOutputInterval(
  simulation = sim,
  startTime = 1440,
  endTime = 1500,
  resolution = 2,
  intervalName = "highResEndSim"
)
sim$outputSchema

<OutputSchema>

── Output intervals ──

<Interval>
  • Name: Simulation Interval 1
  • Start time: 0.00e+00 [min]
  • End time: 15.00 [min]
  • Resolution: 1.00 [pts/min]
<Interval>
  • Name: Simulation Interval 2
  • Start time: 15.00 [min]
  • End time: 1440.00 [min]
  • Resolution: 0.33 [pts/min]
<Interval>
  • Name: Simulation Interval 3
  • Start time: 120.00 [min]
  • End time: 1440.00 [min]
  • Resolution: 0.07 [pts/min]
<Interval>
  • Name: highResEndSim
  • Start time: 1440.00 [min]
  • End time: 1500.00 [min]
  • Resolution: 2.00 [pts/min]

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Clear and replace intervals

clearOutputIntervals(simulation = sim)
sim$outputSchema

<OutputSchema>

── Output intervals ──

# add a new global interval
addOutputInterval(
  simulation = sim,
  startTime = 0,
  endTime = 1440,
  resolution = 0.5,
  intervalName = "globalRes"
)
sim$outputSchema

<OutputSchema>

── Output intervals ──

<Interval>
  • Name: globalRes
  • Start time: 0.00e+00 [min]
  • End time: 1440.00 [min]
  • Resolution: 0.50 [pts/min]

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Inspect solver settings
Numerical solver tolerances + step-size limits live in sim$solver. Tighter tolerances → more accurate but slower; loosen only if runtime is prohibitive and accuracy loss is acceptable.

sim$solver

<SolverSettings>
  • useJacobian: TRUE
  • h0: 1e-10
  • hMin: 0
  • hMax: 60
  • mxStep: 100000
  • relTol: 1e-05
  • absTol: 1e-10

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Change solver settings
Solver fields are writable directly. Typical knobs: absTol, relTol, h0 (initial step), hMin / hMax (step bounds), mxStep (max internal steps).

sim$solver$absTol <- 1e-10
sim$solver$relTol <- 1e-9
sim$solver

<SolverSettings>
  • useJacobian: TRUE
  • h0: 1e-10
  • hMin: 0
  • hMax: 60
  • mxStep: 100000
  • relTol: 1e-09
  • absTol: 1e-10

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
runSimulations
Runs one or many simulations and returns a named list of SimulationResults, keyed by each simulation’s ID (sim$id).

res_list <- runSimulations(sim)
names(res_list) # the simulation IDs

[1] "18VBe6INBEusTVcyKd57mg"

res <- res_list[[1]] # or: res_list[[sim$id]]
res

<SimulationResults>
  • Number of individuals: 1
For paths:
  • Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)
  • Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Whole Blood (Peripheral Venous
  Blood)
  • Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|Aciclovir|Plasma Unbound









Note


Always a list, even for one simulation. Don’t forget [[1]] (or look up by sim$id) to get the actual SimulationResults object.

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Running many simulations
Pass a list of simulations — same return shape, one entry per input.
# CAUTION: do NOT use sim$clone() — ospsuite R6 wraps a .NET object;
# $clone() does not produce an independent simulation. Reload the .pkml instead.
sim_250 <- loadSimulation(simFilePath)
sim_500 <- loadSimulation(simFilePath)

setParameterValuesByPath("Applications|...|Dose", 250, sim_250, units = "mg")
setParameterValuesByPath("Applications|...|Dose", 500, sim_500, units = "mg")

results <- runSimulations(list(sim_250, sim_500))
names(results)               # IDs of sim_250 and sim_500

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Extracting results
getOutputValues() pulls time + numeric values out of a SimulationResults object as a data frame.

outputPath <- "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)"

out <- getOutputValues(res, quantitiesOrPaths = outputPath)

head(out$data) # → time + one column per output path

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
simulationResultsToDataFrame()
Convenience flattener: simulation values + metadata in a single data.frame.

df <- simulationResultsToDataFrame(res)
head(df)


  





Use this when you want one tidy data frame instead of out$data + out$metaData separately.

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Control execution — SimulationRunOptions
runSimulations() accepts a simulationRunOptions argument built with SimulationRunOptions$new(). Tune parallelism, progress bar, and value checks.
opts <- SimulationRunOptions$new(
  numberOfCores         = 4,    # parallel across cores
  showProgress          = TRUE, # progress bar
  checkForNegativeValues = TRUE
)

results <- runSimulations(
  simulations          = list(sim_250, sim_500),
  simulationRunOptions = opts
)






Tip


Parallel scaling is near-linear up to physical core count. Worth it when running many sims or populations; pure overhead for a single short sim.

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Save + load results — CSV
exportResultsToCSV() saves SimulationResults to disk. importResultsFromCSV() reads it back. Useful for slow simulations, sharing with colleagues, or later processing.
# write
exportResultsToCSV(res, "aciclovir_results.csv")

# read back (needs the original Simulation object)
res_loaded <- importResultsFromCSV(sim, "aciclovir_results.csv")






Note


Re-import requires the same Simulation object that produced the results — the CSV stores values but not the model structure.

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Two building blocks

DataSet — container for observed data (manual entry, .pkml, Excel)
DataCombined — links simulated + observed data for plotting



{ospsuite.plots} reads DataCombined → returns ggplot2 objects.

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
DataSet — create
Each DataSet must be named at construction.

dataSet <- DataSet$new("Aciclovir manual")
dataSet$setValues(
  xValues = c(1, 2, 3, 4),
  yValues = c(0, 0.1, 0.6, 10),
  yErrorValues = c(0.001, 0.001, 0.1, 1)
)
dataSetToTibble(dataSet)

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
DataSet — load from .pkml

obs <- loadDataSetFromPKML(
  system.file("extdata", "ObsDataAciclovir_1.pkml", package = "ospsuite")
)
dataSetToTibble(obs) |> head()


  




Excel: loadDataImporterConfiguration() + loadDataSetsFromExcel() — see A6 for the full pattern.

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
DataCombined — assemble

dc <- DataCombined$new()

dc$addSimulationResults(
  simulationResults = res,
  quantitiesOrPaths = outputPath,
  names = "Simulated",
  groups = "Aciclovir"
)

dc$addDataSets(
  obs,
  names = "Observed",
  groups = "Aciclovir"
)

dc$toDataFrame() |> head()

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Five plot types — overview
{ospsuite.plots} provides:



Function
Purpose




plotTimeProfile()
time profiles (sim + obs)


plotPredictedVsObserved()
goodness of fit


plotResidualsVsCovariate()
residuals vs time or predicted


plotResidualsAsHistogram()
residuals distribution


plotQuantileQuantilePlot()
residuals normality (Q-Q)



All return ggplot2 objects — customize with +.

plotTimeProfile(dc)

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling

Customize — ggplot2 layers
All plots are ggplot2 objects. Add layers with +.

library(ggplot2)
plotTimeProfile(dc) +
  labs(
    title = "Aciclovir — single dose",
    subtitle = "Sim vs obs (Vergin 1995)",
    caption = "OSPS R training"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Multi-panel — {patchwork}
| side-by-side · / stacked.

library(patchwork)
tp <- plotTimeProfile(dc)
rt <- plotResidualsVsCovariate(dc, xAxis = "time")
rp <- plotResidualsVsCovariate(dc, xAxis = "predicted")

(tp | rp) / rt + plot_annotation(title = "Aciclovir diagnostics")

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Save a plot — exportPlot()
p <- plotTimeProfile(dc)

ospsuite.plots::exportPlot(
  plotObject = p,
  filePath   = "aciclovir_timeprofile.png",
  width      = 20,   # cm
  height     = NULL, # auto from aspect ratio
  dpi        = 300
)

Returns plain ggplot2 — also works with ggplot2::ggsave(), but exportPlot() handles OSP house style + sizing in cm.

Calculating PK parameters

pk <- calculatePKAnalyses(res)

pkAnalysesAsDataFrame(pk) |>
  dplyr::select(QuantityPath, Parameter, Value, Unit) |>
  head(8)


  


# AUC_inf, C_max, t_max, t_half, ...


Custom PK params: addUserDefinedPKParameter().

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Local sensitivity analysis
Build a SensitivityAnalysis object → vary the listed parameters → run.

gfrPath <- "Neighborhoods|Kidney_pls_Kidney_ur|Aciclovir|Glomerular Filtration-GFR-Aciclovir|GFR fraction"

sa <- SensitivityAnalysis$new(
  simulation = sim,
  parameterPaths = c("Aciclovir|Lipophilicity", gfrPath)
)
sa$variationRange <- 0.1 # ±10 %

saResult <- runSensitivityAnalysis(sa)

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Top influencers — per PK parameter

plasmaPath <- "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)"

saResult$allPKParameterSensitivitiesFor(
  pkParameterName = "AUC_inf",
  outputPath = plasmaPath,
  totalSensitivityThreshold = 1 # show all (default 0.9 hides minor)
)

[[1]]
<PKParameterSensitivity>
  • Parameter name: Aciclovir-Kidney_pls_Kidney_ur-Aciclovir-Glomerular
  Filtration-GFR-Aciclovir-GFR fraction
  • PK-Parameter: AUC_inf
  • Output path: Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral
  Venous Blood)
  • Value: -0.143010911706935

[[2]]
<PKParameterSensitivity>
  • Parameter name: Aciclovir-Lipophilicity
  • PK-Parameter: AUC_inf
  • Output path: Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral
  Venous Blood)

Sensitivity = \(\dfrac{\Delta Y / Y}{\Delta X / X}\) — value 1 means proportional, -1 means inverse.

text:
Scenarios.xlsx — the heart of the workflow

One row per scenario
Columns: Scenario, ModelFile, ApplicationProtocol, IndividualId, PopulationFile, OutputPathsId, SimulationTime, …
ModelParameters references modify any parameter
Hierarchy of overrides — explicit, traceable



ESQapp gives you a GUI to edit these — same files, friendlier UI.

text:
🖥️ Demo — open the project in ESQapp

Switch to ESQapp. Walk through the Midazolam-PAGE project definitions live.


Open Midazolam-PAGE/ProjectConfiguration.json in ESQapp
Tour the Scenarios sheet — model file, protocol, individual, outputs
Open the linked Applications, Individuals, ModelParameters sheets
Show the Plots sheet — DataCombined + plotConfiguration + plotGrids

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Running scenarios
project <- createProjectConfiguration("ProjectConfiguration.xlsx")

# 1. Read the scenario config rows from Scenarios.xlsx
scenarioConfig <- readScenarioConfigurationFromExcel(
  scenarioNames        = c("Midazolam_PO_7.5mg", "Midazolam_IV_2mg"),
  projectConfiguration = project
)

# 2. Materialize Scenario objects (loads .pkml, applies parameters + protocol)
scenarios <- createScenarios(scenarioConfig)

# 3. Run them
results <- runScenarios(scenarios = scenarios)






Note


Two-step: readScenarioConfigurationFromExcel parses Excel rows, createScenarios builds the in-memory Scenario objects from them.

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Scenario Structure





flowchart LR
  S(["Scenario"])
  S --> NAME["scenarioName"]
  S --> MODEL["modelFile<br/>(.pkml)"]
  S --> APP["applicationProtocol<br/><i>Applications.xlsx</i>"]
  S --> PHYS["Physiology"]
  PHYS --> IND["individualId<br/><i>Individuals.xlsx</i>"]
  PHYS --> POP["populationId<br/><i>Populations.xlsx</i>"]
  S --> PARAM["paramSheets<br/><i>ModelParameters.xlsx</i>"]
  S --> OUT["outputPaths"]
  S --> TIME["Simulation time<br/>(start, end, resolution)"]
  S --> SS["Steady-state<br/>(optional)"]







Each property maps to a row or sheet in the configuration Excel files.

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
🖐️ Hands-on 4a — your first scenario (30 min)
Open the Midazolam-PAGE project in ESQapp. Build a new scenario from scratch:

Create a new scenario.
Model file: select Midazolam_PO_7.5mg.pkml.
Administration protocol: create a new protocol — set the dose to 15 mg (everything else as in the 7.5 mg protocol).
Individual: create a new individual — e.g. Female, Black American (pick the matching population).
Simulation time: two intervals — 0–2 h at 60 pts/h, then 2–24 h at 10 pts/h.
Outputs: add Concentration of Midazolam in fat → intracellular: Organism|Fat|Intracellular|Midazolam|Concentration in container.
Save; reload in R via readScenarioConfigurationFromExcel + createScenarios; run via runScenarios.
Access simulated results via $outputValues.


⏰ 30 min · Wilbert circulates

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Parametrization Hierarchy
Parameters are applied top-to-bottom — later layers override earlier ones for the same path.





flowchart TB
  P1["<div style='text-align:center'><b>1. .pkml defaults</b><br/><i>simulation file</i></div>"]
  P2["<div style='text-align:center'><b>2. Individual</b><br/><i>Individuals.xlsx</i></div>"]
  P2a["<div style='text-align:center'><b>2.1 Physiology</b><br/><i>IndividualBiometrics sheet</i></div>"]
  P2b["<div style='text-align:center'><b>2.2 Parameters</b><br/><i>individual-specific sheet</i></div>"]
  P3["<div style='text-align:center'><b>3. Population</b><br/><i>Populations.xlsx / .csv</i></div>"]
  P4["<div style='text-align:center'><b>4. Model parameter sheets</b><br/><i>ModelParameters.xlsx</i><br/>all selected sheets merged;<br/>later sheets overwrite earlier ones</div>"]
  P5["<div style='text-align:center'><b>5. Application protocol</b><br/><i>Applications.xlsx</i></div>"]
  P6["<div style='text-align:center'><b>6. Custom parameters</b><br/><i>customParams</i> arg of <code>createScenarios()</code></div>"]
  P1 --> P2
  P2 --> P2a
  P2 --> P2b
  P2a --> P3
  P2b --> P3
  P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6
  classDef base fill:#eef,stroke:#557,text-align:center;
  classDef top  fill:#fde,stroke:#955,text-align:center;

The final set of parameters applied to the .pkml is available on each scenario via scenarios$<scenarioName>$finalCustomParams.

Defining figures — Plots view
The Plots view in ESQapp has 3 sub-sheets:







Sub-sheet
Purpose

Link simulated results + observed data, define grouping

Plot type, axes, labels, units, scales

Combine plots into multi-panel figures

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Generating the plots in R
Three-step: load observed data → (optionally) build DataCombined → render plots.
# After saving in ESQapp:
restoreProjectConfiguration(file.path(projectDir, "ProjectConfiguration.json"))

# 1. Load observed data from Data/*_TimeValuesData.xlsx
#    using the esqlabs ImporterConfiguration. Pick the sheets you need.
observedData <- loadObservedData(
  projectConfiguration = project,
  sheets               = c("Ahonen 1995", "Allonen 1981")
)

# 2. Optional: explicit DataCombined construction
myDataCombined <- createDataCombinedFromExcel(
  projectConfiguration = project,
  dataCombinedNames    = "Midazolam_PO",
  simulatedScenarios   = results,
  observedData         = observedData
)

# 3. Render plot grids
plots <- createPlotsFromExcel(
  plotGridNames        = "Midazolam_PO_vs_obs",
  simulatedScenarios   = results,
  observedData         = observedData,
  projectConfiguration = project
)

plots$Midazolam_PO_vs_obs






Note


loadObservedData() reads the Excel files in Data/ using Data/esqlabs_dataImporter_configuration.xml. One sheet → one named DataSet. Sheet names must match the dataSet cells in DataCombined.

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Goal of Block 5

Advanced sensitivity analysis · Parameter identification (demo).

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
{esqlabsR} SA — what makes it “advanced”

Multi-factor variationRange (e.g. 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 10) — reveals non-linear / saturation behaviour
Custom output functions (any R function of x time / y output) beyond C_max / t_max / AUC_inf
saveSensitivityCalculation() / loadSensitivityCalculation() — persist expensive runs
Built-in spider, tornado, time-profile plots








vs {ospsuite}::sensitivityAnalysis()


{ospsuite} does local SA — single perturbation per parameter, one index per (parameter, PK metric).

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
SA workflow





flowchart LR
  A["readScenarioConfigurationFromExcel()"] --> B["createScenarios()"]
  B --> C["scenarios$X$simulation"]
  C --> D["sensitivityCalculation()"]
  D --> E1["sensitivitySpiderPlot()"]
  D --> E2["sensitivityTornadoPlot()"]
  D --> E3["sensitivityTimeProfiles()"]

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
SA workflow in code
scenarios <- createScenarios(
  readScenarioConfigurationFromExcel(
    scenarioNames = "Midazolam_PO_7.5mg",
    projectConfiguration = project
  )
)
simulation <- scenarios$Midazolam_PO_7.5mg$simulation

outputPaths <- c(
  Plasma = "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Midazolam|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)"
)
parameterPaths <- c(
  Lipophilicity   = "Midazolam|Lipophilicity",
  CYP3A4_kcat     = "Midazolam-CYP3A4-Optimized|kcat",
  GFR_fraction    = "Neighborhoods|Kidney_pls_Kidney_ur|Midazolam|Glomerular Filtration-Optimized-Midazolam|GFR fraction"
)

analysis <- sensitivityCalculation(
  simulation     = simulation,
  outputPaths    = outputPaths,
  parameterPaths = parameterPaths,
  variationRange = c(0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 10),
  pkParameters   = c("C_max", "t_max", "AUC_inf")
)

sensitivitySpiderPlot(analysis)
sensitivityTornadoPlot(analysis)
sensitivityTimeProfiles(analysis)

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Inspect + persist
head(analysis$pkData)
# cols: ParameterPath, ParameterFactor, ParameterValue,
#       PKParameter, PKParameterValue, PKPercentChange,
#       SensitivityPKParameter

saveSensitivityCalculation(analysis, outputDir = "Results/SA_Midazolam")

# later session — rebuild scenario, then:
analysis <- loadSensitivityCalculation(
  outputDir  = "Results/SA_Midazolam",
  simulation = simulation
)

text:
Demo only — Parameter Identification
library(ospsuite.parameteridentification)

pi_task <- ParameterIdentification$new(
  simulations = list(sim_250, sim_500),
  parameters = list(
    PIParameter$new(parameterPaths = "Aciclovir|Lipophilicity",
                    simulations = list(sim_250, sim_500)),
    PIParameter$new(parameterPaths = "Aciclovir|...|GFR_filtration_fraction",
                    simulations = list(sim_250, sim_500))
  ),
  outputMappings = list(/* sim ↔ obs mappings */),
  configuration = PIConfigurationLeastSquares$new(algorithm = "BOBYQA")
)

result <- pi_task$run()
plot(result)

Full hands-on in C1 of the repo.

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Param ID — what to know

Algorithms: BOBYQA (smooth), HJKB (robust), DEoptim (global)
Objective functions: least-squares (homoscedastic) or M3 (BLOQ-aware)
CIs: Hessian (cheap), profile likelihood (rigorous), bootstrap (gold standard)
Identifiability is the hard problem — check OFV profiles before you trust your fit

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Goal of Block 6

One-button regulatory report from your project.

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
Quarto in 90 seconds

Markdown + R/Python code chunks
Renders to HTML / PDF / Word / reveal.js / docx
Parameters: params: in YAML → re-render with new inputs
Caching, citations, cross-refs, equations — all built in

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
A reproducible M&S report
---
title: "Midazolam PBPK — Single Dose"
params:
  dose_mg: 7.5
  route: "PO"
format:
  html: default
  pdf:
    documentclass: scrreprt
---
# in a chunk — runs scenarios + builds figures + tables
project        <- createProjectConfiguration("ProjectConfiguration.xlsx")
scenarioConfig <- readScenarioConfigurationFromExcel(/* picks scenario from params$dose_mg */)
scenarios      <- createScenarios(scenarioConfig)
results        <- runScenarios(scenarios)

title: R Workflows for advanced PB-QSP Modeling
text:
What goes in the report

Background + objectives (markdown)
Model description (auto-tabulated from Models/)
Scenario specs (auto-table from Scenarios.xlsx)
Simulation results (figure grid via createPlotsFromExcel)
PK parameters (auto-table via calculatePKAnalyses)
Sensitivity summary (load from Results/SA_*.rds)
Conclusions (markdown)



Re-render with quarto render -P dose_mg:15 → fresh PDF.

title: Local Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Load {ospsuite}

library(ospsuite)

title: Local Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Load Sample Simulation

sim <- loadSimulation(system.file(
  "extdata",
  "Aciclovir.pkml",
  package = "ospsuite"
))
clearOutputs(sim)
addOutputs(
  "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)",
  sim
)

title: Local Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Workflow
Steps:

Define parameters to be varied
Create SensitivityAnalysis object
Setup parameter variations



The parameters to vary, that we selected (default: all suitable parameters, here 228)
The range of variation, that we defined (default: 10%)
The number of steps, that we defined (default: 2)

Then, the sensitivity analysis will be performed, and it will give us an idea on how much different PK parameters, such as Cmax or AUC, will vary along with the parameters we selected.
Previously, we defined a variation of 20%, once, for the fraction unbound, lipophilicity and liver volume. We will see how much these variations impact the PK parameters.

title: Local Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Define parameters to be varied
Specific parameters to vary can be defined selectively.
If no parameters are specified, all constant and suitable parameters are automatically varied.
Use potentialVariableParametersFor() to get a list of all suitable parameters.
