title: Local Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Create SensitivityAnalysis object
With all potential parameters:

sa <- SensitivityAnalysis$new(simulation = sim, parameterPaths = params)
sa

<SensitivityAnalysis>
  • Number of steps: 2
  • Variation range: 0.1
  • Number of parameters to vary: 323

title: Local Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Select Parameters
With only some parameters:

sa <- SensitivityAnalysis$new(
  simulation = sim,
  parameterPaths = c(
    "Aciclovir|Lipophilicity",
    "Aciclovir|Fraction unbound (plasma, reference value)"
  )
)
sa

<SensitivityAnalysis>
  • Number of steps: 2
  • Variation range: 0.1
  • Number of parameters to vary: 2

title: Local Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Add Parameters Afterwards






Important


Supplementary parameters can be added to an existing SensitivityAnalysis.




sa$addParameterPaths("Organism|Liver|Volume")

sa

<SensitivityAnalysis>
  • Number of steps: 2
  • Variation range: 0.1
  • Number of parameters to vary: 3








Warning


On an analysis created without parameterPaths (all-params default), addParameterPaths() switches the SA to vary only the newly added parameters.

title: Local Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Setup parameter variations
By default, parameters are incremented/decremented by 10%, twice.
But this can be customized: Here, we will vary the parameters by 20% once.

# One variation per direction (default: 2)
sa$numberOfSteps <- 1

# Variation range (default: 0.1 = 10%)
sa$variationRange <- 0.2

sa

<SensitivityAnalysis>
  • Number of steps: 1
  • Variation range: 0.2
  • Number of parameters to vary: 3









Important


Settings are applied to all parameters.











Note


variationRange is symmetric: 0.2 varies each parameter from -20% to +20% around its baseline.
Total simulations per run = 2 * numberOfSteps * length(parameterPaths).

title: Local Sensitivity Analysis
text:

Simply execute:

saResult <- runSensitivityAnalysis(sa)

title: Local Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Interpret Results
Sensitivity definition:
\[S_{Y,X} = \frac{\Delta Y / Y}{\Delta X / X}\]
Relative change in PK parameter Y per relative change in model parameter X.
Examples:

Variation range set to 0.1 (10%) for parameter X
Returned value of -1 as sensitivity for parameter Y

A variation of 10 % of parameter X leads to a decrease (minus sign) of Y by 10 % (-1 * 10%)

Variation range set to 0.2 (20%) for parameter X
Returned value of 0.25 as sensitivity for parameter Y

A variation of 20 % of parameter X leads to an increase (positive) of Y by 5 % (0.25 * 20%)

title: Local Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Single Sensitivity Value

saResult$pkParameterSensitivityValueFor(
  pkParameterName = "C_max",
  parameterName = "Aciclovir-Fraction unbound (plasma, reference value)",
  outputPath = outputPath
)

[1] -0.7415616



-0.7415616 means that a 20% increase (defined variation range of 0.2) of the fraction unbound in plasma leads to a -15% (-74% * 20%) decrease of \(C_{max}\) at Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood).

title: Local Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Exercise Overview

Create a new sensitivity analysis from scratch
Select 5 parameters (include Dose and Fraction unbound)
Run with defaults, then with variationRange = 0.2
Compare results for C_max and CL

title: Local Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Create the Analysis
Create a new sensitivity analysis from scratch. Select 5 parameters of your choice. Include the Dose and the fraction unbound.


Solution
sim <- loadSimulation(system.file(
  "extdata",
  "Aciclovir.pkml",
  package = "ospsuite"
))

sa <- SensitivityAnalysis$new(
  simulation = sim,
  parameterPaths = c(
    "Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Dose",
    "Aciclovir|Fraction unbound (plasma, reference value)",
    "Organism|Spleen|Volume",
    "Organism|Protein ratio (interstitial/plasma)",
    "Organism|VenousBlood|Volume"
  )
)

title: Local Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Run Two Analyses
Compare two sensitivity analyses. Keep steps and variation range as default for the first, set variationRange to 0.2 for the second, same parameters. Run both.


Solution
sa20 <- SensitivityAnalysis$new(
  simulation = sim,
  parameterPaths = c(
    "Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Dose",
    "Aciclovir|Fraction unbound (plasma, reference value)",
    "Organism|Spleen|Volume",
    "Organism|Protein ratio (interstitial/plasma)",
    "Organism|VenousBlood|Volume"
  )
)

sa20$variationRange <- 0.2

saResult <- runSensitivityAnalysis(sa)
saResult20 <- runSensitivityAnalysis(sa20)

title: Local Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Compare C_max
Compare both results for C_max. Display all parameters (totalSensitivityThreshold = 1).


Solution
outputPath <- saResult$allQuantityPaths[[1]]
outputPath20 <- saResult20$allQuantityPaths[[1]]

# Let's look at the results for Cmax
saResult$allPKParameterSensitivitiesFor(
  pkParameterName = "C_max",
  outputPath = outputPath,
  totalSensitivityThreshold = 1
)
saResult20$allPKParameterSensitivitiesFor(
  pkParameterName = "C_max",
  outputPath = outputPath20,
  totalSensitivityThreshold = 1
)

title: Local Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Compare CL


Solution
# Clearance results
saResult$allPKParameterSensitivitiesFor(
  pkParameterName = "CL",
  outputPath = outputPath,
  totalSensitivityThreshold = 1
)
saResult20$allPKParameterSensitivitiesFor(
  pkParameterName = "CL",
  outputPath = outputPath20,
  totalSensitivityThreshold = 1
)

title: Local Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Interpretation
How do you interpret the results?






Solution



C_max vs Dose is (almost) 1 in both cases: a 10% increase in Dose leads to a 10% increase in C_max, same for 20%
CL vs Fraction unbound is around 0.68 in both cases: 10% increase of Fraction unbound → 10% * 68% = 6.8% increase in CL; 20% → 20% * 68% = 13.6% increase

Sensitivity analysis is “local” and does not consider non-linearities — expect slightly different results for different variation ranges.

title: Creating Individuals
section: 🎯 Goal: Generate virtual individuals from the PK-Sim physiology database and apply them to a simulation
text: 🎯 Goal: Generate virtual individuals from the PK-Sim physiology database and apply them to a simulation

title: Creating Individuals
text:
Overview

Define individual characteristics
Generate parameter values with createIndividual()
Apply parameters to a simulation
Assign enzyme ontogenies

title: Creating Individuals
text:
createIndividualCharacteristics()
Describes the individual before parameter generation.
Required:

species — Species$Human, Species$Rat, Species$Dog, …
population (humans only) — HumanPopulation$...
gender (humans only) — Gender$Male / Gender$Female

Optional:

weight, weightUnit (default "kg")
height, heightUnit (default "m")
age, ageUnit (default "year(s)")
moleculeOntogenies

title: Creating Individuals
text:
Example

library(ospsuite)

individualCharacteristics <- createIndividualCharacteristics(
  species = Species$Human,
  population = HumanPopulation$Japanese_Population,
  gender = Gender$Female,
  weight = 75,
  height = 1.75,
  heightUnit = "m",
  age = 43
)

individualCharacteristics

<IndividualCharacteristics>
  • Species: Human
  • Population: Japanese_Population
  • Gender: FEMALE
  • Age: 43.00 [year(s)]
  • Gestational age: 40.00 [week(s)]
  • Weight: 75.00 [kg]
  • Height: 1.75 [m]

title: Creating Individuals
text:
createIndividual()
PK-Sim draws parameter values matching the characteristics.

individual <- createIndividual(
  individualCharacteristics = individualCharacteristics
)

names(individual)

[1] "distributedParameters" "derivedParameters"     "seed"

title: Creating Individuals
text:
Result Structure

distributedParameters — parameters drawn from physiology distributions ($paths, $values)
derivedParameters — formula-derived parameters (do not set manually)


head(individual$distributedParameters$paths, 3)

[1] "Organism|Age"             "Organism|Gestational age"
[3] "Organism|Height"         

head(individual$distributedParameters$values, 3)

[1] 43.0 40.0 17.5








Warning


Only apply distributedParameters to the simulation. Setting derivedParameters breaks formula dependencies.

title: Creating Individuals
text:
setParameterValuesByPath()

simFilePath <- system.file(
  "extdata",
  "Aciclovir.pkml",
  package = "ospsuite"
)
sim <- loadSimulation(simFilePath)

setParameterValuesByPath(
  parameterPaths = individual$distributedParameters$paths,
  values = individual$distributedParameters$values,
  simulation = sim
)







Important


Species must match the original .pkml. Applying a human individual to a rat simulation runs without error but produces nonsense.

title: Creating Individuals
text:
What Is a Molecule Ontogeny?
Molecule ontogenies describe the development of protein expression during aging.
The expression database stores ontogenies as age-dependent values.
When defining protein ontogenies for individualCharacteristics, the ontogeny value is calculated from the generated individual’s age and stored in the parameters list.

title: Creating Individuals
text:
MoleculeOntogeny
Assign standard ontogeny patterns to custom molecules.

myProteinOntogeny <- MoleculeOntogeny$new(
  molecule = "MyProtein",
  ontogeny = StandardOntogeny$CYP3A4
)

myProteinOntogeny

<MoleculeOntogeny>

  • Ontogeny: CYP3A4

Attach to Characteristics

individualCharacteristics <- createIndividualCharacteristics(
  species = Species$Human,
  population = HumanPopulation$European_ICRP_2002,
  gender = Gender$Male,
  age = 30,
  moleculeOntogenies = list(myProteinOntogeny)

Run the standard Aciclovir simulation (baseline)
Create an Asian female individual, 45 yr, 60 kg, 1.65 m
Apply individual parameters to the simulation and run again
Compare both profiles in one figure

dc <- DataCombined$new()
dc$addSimulationResults(
  simulationResults = resultsBaseline,
  quantitiesOrPaths = outputPath,
  names = "Baseline"
)
dc$addSimulationResults(
  simulationResults = resultsAsian,
  quantitiesOrPaths = outputPath,
  names = "Asian Female 45y"
)

plotTimeProfile(plotData = dc)

title: {ospsuite} Workflow
text:
{ospsuite}
An R package that supports A-Z PK-Sim and MoBi simulation workflow on R.

Load
Manipulate
Run
Visualize
Calculate PK-parameters
Perform (local) sensitivity analysis

title: {ospsuite} Workflow
text:
R6 Reference Semantics
Most {ospsuite} objects (Simulation, Parameter, Molecule, SimulationResults, DataCombined, …) are R6: reference semantics, not base-R copy-on-modify.

Base R (data frames, vectors, lists): copy on modify
R6 objects: mutate in place; x <- y creates an alias, not a copy


simFilePath <- system.file("extdata", "Aciclovir.pkml", package = "ospsuite")
sim <- loadSimulation(simFilePath)

a <- getParameter("Organism|Liver|Interstitial|Volume", sim)
b <- a # NOT a copy — same object

setParameterValues(b, 1.2)
a$value # 1.2 — mutation of b visible on a

[1] 1.2


Need fresh state? Reload the .pkml — {ospsuite} R6 objects are not cloneable.

title: {ospsuite} Workflow
text:
Working With R6 Objects
Access fields/methods with $; construct with ClassName$new():

sim$solver
sim$outputSelections

dc <- DataCombined$new()
dc$addSimulationResults(
  simulationResults = simulationResults,
  quantitiesOrPaths = outputPath
)

Practical rules:

setParameterValues(), addOutputs(), clearOutputs(), addOutputInterval() mutate their argument — no reassignment
Passing an R6 to a function = passing a pointer; the function can change it
reset() on a parameter restores its pkml default without reloading
Fresh independent state: call loadSimulation(path) again

title: {ospsuite} Workflow
text:
Load

simFilePath <- system.file("extdata", "Aciclovir.pkml", package = "ospsuite")
sim <- loadSimulation(simFilePath)
print(sim)

title: {ospsuite} Workflow
text:
Why This Matters
Most {ospsuite} objects (Simulation, Parameter, Molecule, SimulationResults, DataCombined, …) are R6 objects.
R6 behaves differently from base R:

Base R data frames, vectors, lists: copy on modify (functional style).
R6 objects: reference semantics (like Python, Java).

Notice what happened on the previous slide: setParameterValues(param, 0.5) did not return a new parameter. We never reassigned param. Yet the next print(param) shows the new value. The object was mutated in place.

title: {ospsuite} Workflow
text:
Assignment Does Not Copy

a <- getParameter("Organism|Liver|Interstitial|Volume", sim)
b <- a # NOT a copy — b and a point to the same object

setParameterValues(b, 1.2)

a$value # also 1.2 — mutating b mutates a

[1] 1.2


Same applies to sim: sim2 <- sim gives two handles to one simulation. To work on an independent copy, reload the .pkml:

sim_independent <- loadSimulation(simFilePath)
# sim and sim_independent are now fully separate

title: {ospsuite} Workflow
text:
Fields, Methods, and $new()
Access state and behavior with $:

sim$solver # field

<SolverSettings>
  • useJacobian: TRUE
  • h0: 1e-10
  • hMin: 0
  • hMax: 60
  • mxStep: 100000
  • relTol: 1e-05
  • absTol: 1e-10

sim$outputSelections # field

<OutputSelections>
  • Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood):
  Drug, Observer
  • Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|Aciclovir|Plasma Unbound: Drug, Observer


Construct R6 objects with ClassName$new(...):

dc <- DataCombined$new()
sa <- SensitivityAnalysis$new(
  simulation = sim,
  parameterPaths = "Aciclovir|Lipophilicity"
)

Some methods are called via $:

dc$addSimulationResults(
  simulationResults = simulationResults,
  quantitiesOrPaths = outputPath
)

title: {ospsuite} Workflow
text:
Practical Rules

Functions like setParameterValues(), addOutputs(), clearOutputs(), addOutputInterval() mutate their argument. No reassignment needed.
Passing an R6 object to a function is like passing a pointer — the function can change it.
x <- y where y is R6: x is an alias, not a copy.
Need a fresh state? Call loadSimulation(path) again — R6 objects in {ospsuite} are not cloneable.
reset() on a parameter restores its pkml default without reloading the whole simulation.

Run

# Always returns a named list
simulationResults <- runSimulations(sim)[[1]]
print(simulationResults)

head(simulationResultsToDataFrame(simulationResults))

title: Parameterize Simulations
section: 🎯 Goal: Learn how to parameterize a model to perform new simulations using {ospsuite}
text: 🎯 Goal: Learn how to parameterize a model to perform new simulations using {ospsuite}

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
What Can Be Changed
Once a .pkml model file is loaded in {ospsuite}, simulation parameters can be changed from R.
Simulation parameters are accessed and modified using their paths.
Other aspects of the simulation that can be changed include:

Output Paths
Simulation time
Simulation outputs
Solver settings

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Paths
Paths are character strings that represent the hierarchical structure of the simulation.
Each element of a path is separated by |.

# molecule
molecule_path <- "Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|Aciclovir"

# parameter
liver_interstitial_space <- "Organism|Liver|Interstitial|Volume"

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Paths in the Simulation Object
All parameter paths are stored in the simulation object.

library(ospsuite)
simFilePath <- system.file("extdata", "Aciclovir.pkml", package = "ospsuite")

sim <- loadSimulation(simFilePath)

tree <- getSimulationTree(sim)

tree$Organism$PeripheralVenousBlood$Aciclovir$`Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)`

$path
[1] "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)"

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Path Anatomy
Parameter paths are organized in a hierarchical tree structure.

Each layer of a parameter path is a container.
The last element of a parameter path is a parameter, a molecule amount, an observer, or a compartment.

Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Containers
A Simulation object is itself a container. But sub-containers can be created as path shortcuts with getContainer().

tree$Organism$Liver$Interstitial

$Volume
$Volume$path
[1] "Organism|Liver|Interstitial|Volume"


$pH
$pH$path
[1] "Organism|Liver|Interstitial|pH"


$Aciclovir
$Aciclovir$`Concentration in container (Liver, Interstitial)`
$Aciclovir$`Concentration in container (Liver, Interstitial)`$path
[1] "Organism|Liver|Interstitial|Aciclovir|Concentration in container (Liver, Interstitial)"




liver <- getContainer("Organism|Liver", sim)

liver$path

[1] "Organism|Liver"

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Nested Containers

liver_interstitial <- getContainer("Interstitial", liver)

liver_interstitial$path

[1] "Organism|Liver|Interstitial"

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Explore Paths & Containers
{ospsuite} has special functions to explore the containers and paths of a simulation.
getAll*PathsIn()
Return all the containers or parameters paths in a container.

# get all the containers available in "Liver"
getAllContainerPathsIn(liver)[1:5]

[1] "Organism|Liver|Periportal"   "Organism|Liver|Pericentral" 
[3] "Organism|Liver|Plasma"       "Organism|Liver|BloodCells"  
[5] "Organism|Liver|Interstitial"

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Parameter Paths in a Container

getAllParameterPathsIn(liver)[1:5]

[1] "Organism|Liver|Fraction endosomal"           
[2] "Organism|Liver|Weight (tissue)"              
[3] "Organism|Liver|Fluid recirculation flow rate"
[4] "Organism|Liver|Lymph flow rate"              
[5] "Organism|Liver|Volume (endothelium)"

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Match Paths by Pattern
getAll*Matching()
Return all the containers or parameters paths that match a specific pattern.

head(
  getAllContainersMatching("Organism|*|Interstitial", sim),
  3
)

[[1]]
<Container>
  • Container type: Compartment
  • Path: Organism|Bone|Interstitial

[[2]]
<Container>
  • Container type: Compartment
  • Path: Organism|Brain|Interstitial

[[3]]
<Container>
  • Container type: Compartment
  • Path: Organism|Fat|Interstitial

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Match Parameters by Pattern

head(
  getAllParametersMatching("Organism|**|Volume", sim),
  3
)

[[1]]
<Parameter>
  • Quantity Type: Parameter
  • Path: Organism|VenousBlood|Volume
  • Value: 0.91 [l]
  • Value Origin: Publication-ICRP, 2002. Basic Anatomical and Physiological
  Data for Use in Radiological Protection Reference Values. ICRP Publication
  89. Ann. ICRP 32 (3-4). https://doi.org/10.1016/0146-6453(79)90123-4

── Formula ──

  • isDistributed: TRUE

[[2]]
<Parameter>
  • Quantity Type: Parameter
  • Path: Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|Volume
  • Value: 0.50 [l]
  • Value Origin: Publication-Willmann S, Lippert J, Sevestre M, Solodenko J,
  Fois F, Schmitt W. PK-Sim®: a physiologically based pharmacokinetic
  ‘whole-body’ model. Biosilico. 2003; 1 (4): 121-124.
  http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/S1478-5382%2803%2902342-4

── Formula ──

  • isExplicit: TRUE
  • formula: (1-HCT)*V
  • Value overrides formula: FALSE

[[3]]
<Parameter>
  • Quantity Type: Parameter
  • Path: Organism|VenousBlood|BloodCells|Volume
  • Value: 0.41 [l]
  • Value Origin: Publication-Willmann S, Lippert J, Sevestre M, Solodenko J,
  Fois F, Schmitt W. PK-Sim®: a physiologically based pharmacokinetic
  ‘whole-body’ model. Biosilico. 2003; 1 (4): 121-124.
  http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/S1478-5382%2803%2902342-4

── Formula ──

  • isExplicit: TRUE
  • formula: HCT*V
  • Value overrides formula: FALSE


It works in tandem with a special path syntax including wildcards (*).

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Wildcards
** is a wildcard that matches any structure of path elements.
In the example below, it means that we are looking for any number of containers between Organism and Interstitial.

# get all the containers that have an Interstitial space with any amount of layers in between
interstitial_containers <- getAllContainersMatching(
  "Organism|**|Interstitial",
  sim
)
length(interstitial_containers)

[1] 28




interstitial_containers[[1]]
interstitial_containers[[22]]
interstitial_containers[[9]]

<Container>
  • Container type: Compartment
  • Path: Organism|Bone|Interstitial
<Container>
  • Container type: Compartment
  • Path: Organism|Liver|Periportal|Interstitial
<Container>
  • Container type: Compartment
  • Path: Organism|SmallIntestine|Mucosa|Duodenum|Interstitial

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Single-Element Wildcard
* is a wildcard that matches exactly one path element.

# get all the containers that have an Interstitial with exactly one path element before "Interstitial"
interstitial_containers_1 <- getAllContainersMatching(
  "Organism|*|Interstitial",
  sim
)
interstitial_containers_1[[1]]

<Container>
  • Container type: Compartment
  • Path: Organism|Bone|Interstitial




# get all the containers that have an Interstitial with exactly two path elements before "Interstitial"
interstitial_containers_2 <- getAllContainersMatching(
  "Organism|*|*|Interstitial",
  sim
)
interstitial_containers_2[[1]]

<Container>
  • Container type: Compartment
  • Path: Organism|Liver|Periportal|Interstitial





# get all the containers that have an Interstitial with exactly three path elements before "Interstitial"
interstitial_containers_3 <- getAllContainersMatching(
  "Organism|*|*|*|Interstitial",
  sim
)
interstitial_containers_3[[1]]

<Container>
  • Container type: Compartment
  • Path: Organism|SmallIntestine|Mucosa|Duodenum|Interstitial

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Get Parameter Values
Parameter values can be read from the simulation with getParameter().

getParameter("Organism|Liver|Interstitial|Volume", sim)

<Parameter>
  • Quantity Type: Parameter
  • Path: Organism|Liver|Interstitial|Volume
  • Value: 0.35 [l]
  • Value Origin: Publication-Willmann S, Lippert J, Sevestre M, Solodenko J,
  Fois F, Schmitt W. PK-Sim®: a physiologically based pharmacokinetic
  ‘whole-body’ model. Biosilico. 2003; 1 (4): 121-124.
  http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/S1478-5382%2803%2902342-4

── Formula ──

  • isExplicit: TRUE
  • formula: f_int*V
  • Value overrides formula: FALSE



It can also be assigned for future access.

liver_int_volume <- getParameter("Organism|Liver|Interstitial|Volume", sim)

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Sub-container Shortcut







Tip: Identical results with sub-containers


getParameter("Interstitial|Volume", liver)
getParameter("Volume", liver_interstitial)

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Set Parameters Values
Once a get...() function is used, the resulting object can be used to change the value of the parameter or molecule initial values.

ageParam <- getParameter("Organism|Age", sim)
ageParam

<Parameter>
  • Quantity Type: Parameter
  • Path: Organism|Age
  • Value: 25.70 [year(s)]

── Formula ──

  • isConstant: TRUE




setParameterValues(ageParam, 55, units = "year(s)")
ageParam

<Parameter>
  • Quantity Type: Parameter
  • Path: Organism|Age
  • Value: 55.00 [year(s)]

── Formula ──

  • isConstant: TRUE

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Set Values by Path
setParameterValuesByPath() is a shortcut that combines getParameter() and setParameterValues() into a single call. It accepts either a single path or a vector of paths.

setParameterValuesByPath(
  parameterPaths = c("Organism|Age", "Organism|Weight"),
  values = c(40, 75),
  simulation = sim
)

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Reset Parameter Values
Original values can be restored with reset().

ageParam$reset()
ageParam

<Parameter>
  • Quantity Type: Parameter
  • Path: Organism|Age
  • Value: 25.70 [year(s)]

── Formula ──

  • isConstant: TRUE

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Change Simulation outputs
The simulations outputs are set based on what is defined in the imported .pkml file.

sim$outputSelections

<OutputSelections>
  • Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood):
  Drug, Observer
  • Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|Aciclovir|Plasma Unbound: Drug, Observer



Other simulation outputs can be added with addOutputs()

addOutputs(
  "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Whole Blood (Peripheral Venous Blood)",
  simulation = sim
)

sim$outputSelections

<OutputSelections>
  • Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood):
  Drug, Observer
  • Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|Aciclovir|Plasma Unbound: Drug, Observer
  • Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Whole Blood (Peripheral Venous
  Blood): Drug, Observer

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Clear Simulation Outputs
All simulation outputs can be removed with clearOutputs().

clearOutputs(sim)
# The outputs are now empty, we then add the original one

addOutputs(
  "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)",
  simulation = sim
)

sim$outputSelections

<OutputSelections>
  • Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood):
  Drug, Observer

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Change Simulation time
Default simulation time is the one defined in the imported .pkml file.

sim$outputSchema

<OutputSchema>

── Output intervals ──

<Interval>
  • Name: Simulation Interval 1
  • Start time: 0.00e+00 [min]
  • End time: 15.00 [min]
  • Resolution: 1.00 [pts/min]
<Interval>
  • Name: Simulation Interval 2
  • Start time: 15.00 [min]
  • End time: 1440.00 [min]
  • Resolution: 0.33 [pts/min]
<Interval>
  • Name: Simulation Interval 3
  • Start time: 120.00 [min]
  • End time: 1440.00 [min]
  • Resolution: 0.07 [pts/min]

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Add an Output Interval
Another OutputSchema can be set with addOutputInterval().

addOutputInterval(
  simulation = sim,
  startTime = 1440,
  endTime = 1500,
  resolution = 2,
  intervalName = "highResEndSim"
)
sim$outputSchema

<OutputSchema>

── Output intervals ──

<Interval>
  • Name: Simulation Interval 1
  • Start time: 0.00e+00 [min]
  • End time: 15.00 [min]
  • Resolution: 1.00 [pts/min]

  • Start time: 15.00 [min]
  • End time: 1440.00 [min]

<Interval>

  • Start time: 120.00 [min]
  • End time: 1440.00 [min]

<Interval>

  • Start time: 1440.00 [min]
  • End time: 1500.00 [min]

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Clear and Replace Intervals
All output intervals can be removed with the clearOutputIntervals() function.

clearOutputIntervals(simulation = sim)
sim$outputSchema

<OutputSchema>

── Output intervals ──

#we add a new global interval
addOutputInterval(
  simulation = sim,
  startTime = 0,
  endTime = 1440,
  resolution = 0.5,
  intervalName = "globalRes"
)
sim$outputSchema

<OutputSchema>

<Interval>

  • Start time: 0.00e+00 [min]

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Inspect Solver Settings
Numerical solver tolerances and step-size limits are stored in sim$solver. Tighter tolerances improve accuracy at the cost of runtime; loosen them only when runtime is prohibitive and accuracy loss is acceptable.

sim$solver

<SolverSettings>
  • useJacobian: TRUE
  • h0: 1e-10
  • hMin: 0
  • hMax: 60
  • mxStep: 100000
  • relTol: 1e-05
  • absTol: 1e-10

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Change Solver Settings
Solver fields are writable directly. Typical knobs: absTol (absolute tolerance), relTol (relative tolerance), h0 (initial step), hMin / hMax (step bounds), mxStep (maximum internal steps).

sim$solver$absTol <- 1e-10
sim$solver$relTol <- 1e-9
sim$solver

<SolverSettings>
  • useJacobian: TRUE
  • h0: 1e-10
  • hMin: 0
  • hMax: 60
  • mxStep: 100000
  • relTol: 1e-09
  • absTol: 1e-10

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Exercise Overview
Walk through a full iterative modeling loop:

Load the simulation. Reload the Aciclovir .pkml into a fresh sim object.
Find the application protocol paths. List every parameter path under Events.
Find the Dose parameter paths. Filter the Events paths down to the Dose parameters.
Change the Dose. Create sim_100mg and set the dose to 100 mg.
Change the administration protocol. Set the infusion time to 30 minutes and verify the new value.
Change the simulation time. Replace the output intervals with a finer-then-coarser resolution schedule.

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Load Simulation
Let’s load again the simulation in a new and fresh object called “sim”

simFilePath <- system.file("extdata", "Aciclovir.pkml", package = "ospsuite")

sim <- loadSimulation(simFilePath)

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Find the Application protocol paths
Let’s have a look at the application protocol parameters

Find the parameter paths that start with Events



Answer
getAllParametersMatching("Events|**|*", sim)

# ** for any number of structure layer
# * for any parameter path endpoint

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Find the Dose parameter paths
Now, we anticipate that we will have to change the dose in our simulation. So we will need to identify all the Dose parameters within Events.

Find all paths that start with Events and end with any kind of Dose







Tip


To look for all the Dose word, look for *Dose* in the path so that it will be found even if the word is surrounded by other characters.





Answer
getAllParametersMatching("Events|**|*Dose*", sim)

# ** for any number of structure layer
# * for any parameter path endpoint
# *Dose* to match any string containing "Dose".

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Change the Dose
Now, we will create another simulation with a different dose. Create one simulation object, sim_100mg, in which you will set the dose to 100 mg.






Tip


Once you have your sim object, you can either: - use getParameter() then setParameterValues(), or - use setParameterValuesByPath() directly as a shortcut





Answer
sim_100mg <- loadSimulation(simFilePath)

# Option 1: getParameter + setParameterValues
dose_100mg <- getParameter(
  "Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Dose",
  sim_100mg
)
setParameterValues(dose_100mg, 100, units = "mg")

# Option 2: setParameterValuesByPath
setParameterValuesByPath(
  parameterPaths = "Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Dose",
  values = 100,
  units = "mg",
  simulation = sim_100mg
)

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Change Administration protocol
Now, we need to set the drug infusion time to 30 minutes for our simulation.

Find the parameter path for the Infusion time parameter in the Applications section.
Set the parameter to 30 minutes.
Get the value of the parameter to check if it has been changed.



Answer
# 1) Infusion Time change
InfusionTime <- getParameter(
  "Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Infusion time",
  sim_100mg
)
# 2)
setParameterValues(InfusionTime, 30, units = "min")

# 3)
getParameter(
  "Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Infusion time",
  sim_100mg
)$value

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Change simulation time
We anticipate that the parameters we changed will mainly impact the first minutes of the simulation. So we want to increase the resolution for the first part of the simulation.

Clear all previously defined outputIntervals
Add a new interval from 0 to 15 minutes with 2 points per minutes.
Add a new interval from 15 to 120 minutes with 1 point each 2 minutes
Extend the simulation from 120 to 1440 minutes with 1 point each 15 minutes

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Change simulation time — Answers 1 & 2


Answers 1 & 2
# 1)
clearOutputIntervals(simulation = sim_100mg)

# 2)
addOutputInterval(
  simulation = sim_100mg,
  startTime = 0,
  endTime = 15,
  resolution = 2,
  intervalName = "2 points per minute"
)

title: Parameterize Simulations
text:
Change simulation time — Answers 3 & 4


Answers 3 & 4
# 3)
addOutputInterval(
  simulation = sim_100mg,
  startTime = 15,
  endTime = 120,
  resolution = 0.5,
  intervalName = "1 point each 2 minutes"
)
# 4)
addOutputInterval(
  simulation = sim_100mg,
  startTime = 120,
  endTime = 1440,
  resolution = 0.07,
  intervalName = "1 point each 15 minutes"
)

title: Plot Simulation Results
text:
Simulated and Observed Data
Comparing simulated and observed data is crucial in modeling and simulation workflow.
{ospsuite} provides a set of features to handle these data before visualizing them.

DataSet to import and store observed data,
DataCombined to link observed and simulated data

title: Plot Simulation Results
text:
DataSet
In {ospsuite} observed data are stored in DataSet objects.
Standard format for data coming from:

Manual entry,
.pkml files,
Excel files

title: Plot Simulation Results

Create DataSet in R
Each DataSet must be named when the object is created.

dataSet <- DataSet$new("Aciclovir")
dataSet

text:
Import DataSet from Excel File
Loading observed data from an Excel sheet requires an ImporterConfiguration object.
ImporterConfiguration describes the mapping of the columns and metadata of the Excel sheet.
Can be created in PK-Sim or MoBi (.xml file) and loaded in R using the loadDataImporterConfiguration() function.

configurationFilePath <- system.file(

Add several Observed Data

myDataCombined2 <- DataCombined$new()

obsData <- lapply(
  c(
    "ObsDataAciclovir_1.pkml",
    "ObsDataAciclovir_2.pkml",
    "ObsDataAciclovir_3.pkml"
  ),
  function(x) {
    loadDataSetFromPKML(system.file("extdata", x, package = "ospsuite"))
  }
)

names(obsData) <- lapply(obsData, function(x) x$name)

myDataCombined2$addDataSets(
  dataSets = c(obsData$`Vergin 1995.Iv`, obsData$`Laskin 1982.Group C`),
  names = c("Observed (Vergin 1995.Iv)", "Observed (Laskin 1982.Group C)"),

title: Plot Simulation Results
text:
Generate Plots
{ospsuite.plots} provides five plotting functions. All return ggplot2 objects.

plotTimeProfile() — time profiles
plotPredictedVsObserved() — goodness of fit
plotResidualsVsCovariate() — residuals against time, observed, or predicted
plotResidualsAsHistogram() — residuals distribution
plotQuantileQuantilePlot() — residuals normality check








setDefaults()


ospsuite.plots::setDefaults() (called in the setup chunk) installs the OSP house style: color palettes, linear/log scales, legend placement, and watermark settings. Call it once per session before plotting.

title: Plot Simulation Results
text:
Time Profile

plotTimeProfile(myDataCombined)

title: Plot Simulation Results
text:
Time Profile — Log Scale
Plasma profiles are typically read on a log scale. Pass yScale = "log".

plotTimeProfile(myDataCombined, yScale = "log")

title: Plot Simulation Results
text:
Predicted vs Observed

plotPredictedVsObserved(myDataCombined)

title: Plot Simulation Results
text:
Residuals vs Predicted

plotResidualsVsCovariate(myDataCombined, xAxis = "predicted")

title: Plot Simulation Results
text:
Residuals vs Time

plotResidualsVsCovariate(myDataCombined, xAxis = "time")

title: Plot Simulation Results
text:
Residuals Histogram

plotResidualsAsHistogram(myDataCombined)

title: Plot Simulation Results
text:
Residuals Q-Q Plot

plotQuantileQuantilePlot(myDataCombined)

title: Plot Simulation Results
text:
Customize Plots
All plotting functions return ggplot2 objects, so customization uses the standard ggplot2 + operator. ggplot2 builds plots in layers: each + adds something new (a title, a theme, axis limits, etc.). A quick reference: ggplot2.tidyverse.org

library(ggplot2)

plotTimeProfile(myDataCombined) +
  labs(
    title = "Example: Customize a Plot",
    subtitle = "Using ggplot2 layers",
    caption = "Source: Aciclovir data"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

title: Plot Simulation Results
text:
Multi-Panel with {patchwork}
{ospsuite.plots} returns plain ggplot2 objects. Combine them with {patchwork} operators +, |, /; | places plots side by side, / stacks them vertically.

library(patchwork)

timeProfile <- plotTimeProfile(myDataCombined)
resVsPred <- plotResidualsVsCovariate(myDataCombined, xAxis = "predicted")
resVsTime <- plotResidualsVsCovariate(myDataCombined, xAxis = "time")

((timeProfile | resVsPred) / resVsTime) +
  plot_annotation(title = "Multiple plots in one figure")

title: Plot Simulation Results
text:
Save Plots
Use ospsuite.plots::exportPlot() to write a plot to disk.

p <- plotTimeProfile(myDataCombined)

ospsuite.plots::exportPlot(
  plotObject = p,
  filePath = "resources/A6/exportedPlot.png",
  width = 20,
  height = NULL,
  dpi = 300
)

title: Plot Simulation Results
text:
Run Simulations

Load ospsuite and the aciclovir model in two separate simulation objects called sim and sim_100mg
Modify the Dose parameter in sim_100mg to 100 mg
Run the simulations and assign the output to results



Answer
library(ospsuite)
simFilePath <- system.file("extdata", "Aciclovir.pkml", package = "ospsuite")

sim <- loadSimulation(simFilePath)
sim_100mg <- loadSimulation(simFilePath)

setParameterValuesByPath(
  simulation = sim_100mg,
  parameterPaths = "Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Dose",
  values = 100,
  units = "mg"
)

results <- runSimulations(simulations = list(sim = sim, sim_100mg = sim_100mg))

text:
Add Simulation Results

Add the simulation results to myDataCombined and specify that the simulation results name is Simulated and that it belongs to the Aciclovir group.
Plot the time profile for myDataCombined.







Tip


The default pkml exposes more than one output path. When passing a single name, also pass quantitiesOrPaths to restrict to one output — the vector lengths must match.





Answer
plasmaPath <- "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)"

myDataCombined$addSimulationResults(
  simulationResults = results[[1]],
  quantitiesOrPaths = plasmaPath,
  names = "Simulated",
  groups = "Aciclovir"
)

plotTimeProfile(myDataCombined)

section: 🎯 Goal: Learn how to reduce computational time and code repetitions
text: 🎯 Goal: Learn how to reduce computational time and code repetitions

title: Simulation Optimizations
text:
Simulation Life Cycle
A typical simulation workflow includes the following steps.

title: Simulation Optimizations
text:
Performances: Factors to Consider


Long loading and/or initialization

Model complexity
Scenario complexity (number of compounds)

Long execution time

Model complexity
Scenario time span
Scenario complexity (number of administrations)
Solver settings

title: Simulation Optimizations
text: Load, Set, Run

title: Simulation Optimizations
text:
Load, Set, Run — Code

library(ospsuite)

# Load the simulation
sim <- loadSimulation(system.file(
  "extdata",
  "Aciclovir.pkml",
  package = "ospsuite"
))

# Change Dose Parameter
doseParameter <-
  getParameter(
    path = "Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Dose",
    container = sim
  )

doseParameter$value <- toBaseUnit(doseParameter, 100, "mg")

# Run Simulation
results <- runSimulations(simulations = sim)[[1]]

title: Simulation Optimizations
text:
About Single Simulations

Easy to implement
Linear process
Runs only one simulation/scenario

title: Simulation Optimizations
text: Sequential Runs

title: Simulation Optimizations
text:
Sequential Runs — Code

library(ospsuite)

# Load the simulation
sim <- loadSimulation(system.file(
  "extdata",
  "Aciclovir.pkml",
  package = "ospsuite"
))

# Change Dose Parameter
doseParameter <-
  getParameter(
    path = "Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Dose",
    container = sim
  )

# Simulation 1
doseParameter$value <- toBaseUnit(doseParameter, 100, "mg")
results_100mg <- runSimulations(simulations = sim)[[1]]

# Simulation 2
doseParameter$value <- toBaseUnit(doseParameter, 200, "mg")
results_200mg <- runSimulations(simulations = sim)[[1]]

title: Simulation Optimizations
text:
About Iterative Simulations

Simulations are run sequentially
The simulation object is altered along the way
Simulation is loaded only once
Easy to implement and understand


Most useful for quick and easy run of simple and few simulations and scenarios.

title: Simulation Optimizations
text: Load Twice, Run in Parallel

title: Simulation Optimizations
section: Load Twice, Run in Parallel — Code
text:
Load Twice, Run in Parallel — Code

simulation_file <- system.file(
  "extdata",
  "Aciclovir.pkml",
  package = "ospsuite"
)

# Initialize simulation with dose set to 100mg
sim100 <- loadSimulation(simulation_file)

setParameterValuesByPath(
  parameterPaths = "Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Dose",
  simulation = sim100,
  value = toBaseUnit("Mass", 100, "mg")
)

# Initialize simulation with dose set to 200mg
sim200 <- loadSimulation(simulation_file)

setParameterValuesByPath(
  parameterPaths = "Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Dose",
  simulation = sim200,
  value = toBaseUnit("Mass", 200, "mg")
)

# Run simulations in parallel
results <- runSimulations(simulations = list(sim100, sim200))

title: Simulation Optimizations
text:
About Parallel Simulations

Reduce simulation time (depending on the number of cores)
Each simulation scenario is independent of the others
One output: named list of SimulationResults objects.


Most useful when running several different simulations loaded from different files.

title: Simulation Optimizations
text: Batch Workflow

title: Simulation Optimizations
text:
Freeze Structure, Vary Parameters







Define variable parameters at batch creation


Parameters or start values that can be varied later must be defined at the time of batch creation. All other parameters are “frozen” and cannot be changed later.

title: Simulation Optimizations
text:
Create and Run a Batch

parameterPaths <- c("Aciclovir|Lipophilicity", "Aciclovir|Permeability")

simBatch <- createSimulationBatch(
  simulation = sim,
  parametersOrPaths = parameterPaths
)

# Set parameters
# First value changes Lipophilicity, second Permeability
# Each call to addRunValues adds a new simulation run
# Each run gets a unique ID that is returned
simBatch$addRunValues(parameterValues = c(1, 2))

[1] "0db0ec33-37b5-4371-b8f1-02479ad516ff"

simBatch$addRunValues(parameterValues = c(3, 4))

[1] "9e7cf9d5-800d-4a3c-aa38-b5088d78a527"

simBatch$addRunValues(parameterValues = c(5, 6))

[1] "8008da93-f98f-4238-b63b-92fd1c5db113"

results <- runSimulationBatches(simBatch)

text:
Nested Result List
results is a nested named list of SimulationResults objects. The names of the first level are IDs of the simulation batches:

print(names(results))

[1] "657c3f79-0c68-4cdb-87ad-ebd754e71a80"

print(simBatch$id)

[1] "657c3f79-0c68-4cdb-87ad-ebd754e71a80"



The names of the second level are the IDs of the runs added to that simulation batch:

print(names(results[[simBatch$id]]))

[1] "0db0ec33-37b5-4371-b8f1-02479ad516ff"
[2] "9e7cf9d5-800d-4a3c-aa38-b5088d78a527"
[3] "8008da93-f98f-4238-b63b-92fd1c5db113"

text:
Run IDs and Clearing






Note


Each run gets a unique ID which are used to access results in the result list

print(names(unlist(results)))

[1] "657c3f79-0c68-4cdb-87ad-ebd754e71a80.0db0ec33-37b5-4371-b8f1-02479ad516ff"

Note


Run Values are cleared when calling runSimulationBatches()

title: Simulation Optimizations
text:
About Simulation Batches

Load and initialize a simulation only once,
Undefined parameters cannot be changed later,
Reduce simulation time (depending on the number of cores available),
One output: named list of SimulationResults objects.


Most useful for running several scenarios of the same simulation.

title: Simulation Optimizations
text:
Exercise Overview
Practice the three approaches on the same scenario (default 250 mg vs 100 mg dose):

Sequential. Alter one simulation in place, run twice.
Parallel. Load two simulations, run them together with runSimulations().
Batch. Freeze structure with createSimulationBatch(), vary dose via addRunValues().
Two-parameter batch. Vary dose and infusion time together.

title: Simulation Optimizations
text:
Sequential Baseline
In the second lecture, you have created two objects: simExercise and simDose100.
Here is a reminder of what it looked like:

library(ospsuite)

# Simulation Setup
simFilePath <- system.file("extdata", "Aciclovir.pkml", package = "ospsuite")

simExercise <- loadSimulation(simFilePath)
simDose100 <- loadSimulation(simFilePath)

Dose100 <- getParameter(
  "Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Dose",
  simDose100
)
setParameterValues(Dose100, 100, units = "mg")

# Simulations are run
simExercise_res <- runSimulations(simulations = simExercise)
simDose100_res <- runSimulations(simulations = simDose100)



Loading time: 1.518 secs
Simulation time: 2.506 secs.

title: Simulation Optimizations
text:
Parallel Exercise
Now is your turn! Try to do the same, but using the “parallel” approach.


Solution
simFilePath <- system.file("extdata", "Aciclovir.pkml", package = "ospsuite")

simExercise <- loadSimulation(simFilePath)
simDose100 <- loadSimulation(simFilePath)

setParameterValuesByPath(
  parameterPaths = "Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Dose",
  simulation = simDose100,
  value = toBaseUnit("Mass", 100, "mg")
)

# We run the simulations in parallel
results <- runSimulations(simulations = list(simExercise, simDose100))

title: {esqlabsR} Workflow
text: 🎯 Goal: Understand {esqlabsR} structure and workflow

title: {esqlabsR} Workflow
text:
PKSim/MoBi vs {ospsuite}


PKSim/MoBi

Graphical Interface,
No programming skills required,
Cannot be 100% automated,
No version control,
Harder to reproduce.


{ospsuite}

Can be 100% automated,
Low-level access to the models,
Easy to reproduce,
Programming skills required.




Need a bridge: keep the GUI ergonomics, gain reproducibility.

title: {esqlabsR} Workflow
text:
{esqlabsR}
{esqlabsR} bridges the two approaches

Scenario-centric workflow,
From scenario definition to report generation,
Graphical interface (Excel files, ESQapp, or agentic (in development)),
Versionable,
100% reproducible.

title: {esqlabsR} Workflow
text:
How does it work?
{esqlabsR} is based on:

A 7 steps workflow supported by a set of easy-to-use functions,
A collection of excel files that serve as input sources for the workflow.
JSON representation of the project for programmatic processing.

title: {esqlabsR} Workflow
text:
{esqlabsR}’s Workflow



Project Initialization (once per project)
Scenario Design
Specify Figures
Specify Parameter Identification (optional)



Perform Sensitivity Analysis (optional)
Execute tasks
Generate Markdown Report

title: {esqlabsR} Workflow
text:
Excel Files
{esqlabsR}’s workflow relies on a collection of excel files with predefined structures. These are the main interfaces for the user to define simulation scenarios.
 Project Folder
 ├── ...
 └── Configurations
     ├── PopulationsCSV
     │   └── TestPopulation.csv
     ├── Applications.xlsx
     ├── Individuals.xlsx
     ├── ModelParameters.xlsx
     ├── ParameterIdentification.xlsx
     ├── Plots.xlsx
     ├── Populations.xlsx
     └── Scenarios.xlsx

title: {esqlabsR} Workflow
text:
Desktop Application
{esqlabsR} also provides a desktop application (ESQapp) to interact with the workflow in a more user-friendly way.

title: {esqlabsR} Workflow
text:
Step 1: Project Initialization and structure
{esqlabsR} relies on a predefined project structure that keeps the project organized.


 Project Folder
 ├── Configurations
 ├── Data
 ├── Models
 │   └── Simulations
 ├── Results
 ├── ProjectConfiguration.json
 └── ProjectConfiguration.xlsx


4 folders,
7 configuration files,
ProjectConfiguration.xlsx + ProjectConfiguration.json to link them all.



Standardized but can be customized.

title: {esqlabsR} Workflow
text:
Step 2: Scenario Design
A scenario is defined by:

The simulation file containing the model structure,
Parameterization of the model,
Application protocol,
Physiology of the simulated individual or population.


All these elements are defined in the Excel files.

title: {esqlabsR} Workflow
text:
Step 2: Scenario Structure





flowchart LR
  S(["Scenario"])
  S --> NAME["scenarioName"]
  S --> MODEL["modelFile<br/>(.pkml)"]
  S --> APP["applicationProtocol<br/><i>Applications.xlsx</i>"]
  S --> PHYS["Physiology"]
  PHYS --> IND["individualId<br/><i>Individuals.xlsx</i>"]
  PHYS --> POP["populationId<br/><i>Populations.xlsx</i>"]
  S --> PARAM["paramSheets<br/><i>ModelParameters.xlsx</i>"]
  S --> OUT["outputPaths"]
  S --> TIME["Simulation time<br/>(start, end, resolution)"]
  S --> SS["Steady-state<br/>(optional)"]







Each property maps to a row or sheet in the configuration Excel files.

title: {esqlabsR} Workflow
text:
Step 3: Specify Figures
Figures are defined in Plots.xlsx and generated automatically when scenarios run.





flowchart LR
  P(["Plots.xlsx"])
  P --> DC["DataCombined"]
  DC --> DCN["DataCombinedName"]
  DC --> DT["dataType"]
  P --> PC["plotConfiguration"]
  PC --> PID["plotID"]
  PC --> PT["plotType<br/>(individual / population /<br/>observedVsSimulated /<br/>residualsVsSimulated /<br/>residualsVsTime)"]
  PC --> AX["axes / scales / limits<br/>(optional)"]
  P --> PG["plotGrids"]
  PG --> GN["name"]
  PG --> GP["plotIDs"]
  PG --> GT["title"]

title: {esqlabsR} Workflow
text:
Step 4: Specify Parameter Identification (optional)
Three-step PI workflow:

PI tasks are built on configured scenarios.
Specify scenarios, parameters to estimate, and observed data in ParameterIdentification.xlsx.

title: {esqlabsR} Workflow
text:
PI Excel Configuration





flowchart LR
  PI(["ParameterIdentification.xlsx"])
  PI --> PP["<b>PIParameters</b><br/><i>required</i><br/>parameters, bounds,<br/>start values, group"]
  PI --> POM["<b>PIOutputMappings</b><br/><i>required</i><br/>map sim outputs to<br/>observed data,<br/>scaling, weights"]
  PI --> PC["<b>PIConfiguration</b><br/><i>optional</i><br/>algorithm, CI method,<br/>error model"]
  PI --> AO["<b>AlgorithmOptions</b><br/><i>optional</i><br/>optimizer fine-tuning"]
  PI --> CIO["<b>CIOptions</b><br/><i>optional</i><br/>CI options"]







Same Group value in PIParameters shares one parameter estimate across scenarios.

title: {esqlabsR} Workflow
text:
Step 5: Perform Sensitivity Analysis (optional)


Automated re-runs with scaled parameters (default 0.1×–10×). Ranks parameters by influence on PK metrics (C_max, t_max, AUC_inf, …).
R function: sensitivityCalculation().
Output: spider, time-profile, and tornado plots.


Spider plot: PK parameter response across scaled inputs (esqlabsR vignette).

Sensitivity Analysis vignette

title: {esqlabsR} Workflow
text:
Step 6: Execute
Scenarios are simulated, PI tasks run, SA performed, and figures generated based on the configurations defined in the Excel files.

Subsets can be selected (only some scenarios / PI tasks / plots).
Results can be saved and loaded later.

title: {esqlabsR} Workflow
text:
Step 7: Generate Markdown Report
Quarto templates using the project configuration.
Pick scenarios, plot grids, and data sheets — createResults() runs the simulations and builds the figures:
projectConfiguration <- createProjectConfiguration(
  here::here("ProjectConfiguration.xlsx")
)

reportResults <- createResults(
  projectConfiguration = projectConfiguration,
  scenarioNames        = c("Scenario_A", "Scenario_B"),
  dataSheets           = c("ObservedData"),
  plotGridNames        = c("PK_overview")
)

Embed a generated figure by referring to its plotID:
#| label: fig-pk-overview
#| fig-cap: "PK overview for Scenario A & B."
reportResults$plots$PK_overview

title: Scenario Design
text: 🎯 Goal: Create custom simulation scenarios

title: Scenario Design
text:
Scenarios
In the {esqlabsR} framework, simulations are run by defining and executing Scenarios.

A Scenario takes the following inputs:

Simulation file (model structure),
Model parameters,
Application protocol,
Individual and/or population physiology.

title: Scenario Design
text:
Step-wise Workflow
The {esqlabsR} framework follows a step-wise approach.

title: Scenario Design
text:
Step 1: Modeling

Model development in PK-Sim/MoBi.
Store the .pkml file in the dedicated project folder (Models/Simulations/ by default).

myProject/Models/
└── Simulations/
    └── Aciclovir.pkml

title: Scenario Design
text:
Step 2: Scenario Initialization
A new simulation scenario is created in Scenarios.xlsx (in Configurations/ by default).
myProject/Configurations/
├── Applications.xlsx
├── Individuals.xlsx
├── ModelParameters.xlsx
├── ParameterIdentification.xlsx
├── Plots.xlsx
├── Populations.xlsx
├── PopulationsCSV/
│   └── TestPopulation.csv
└── Scenarios.xlsx






Tip


Run validateAllConfigurations(projectConfiguration) to sanity-check the Excel files (see B2).

text:
Scenario Name
The Scenario_name column must hold a new unique name.

Scenarios

title: Scenario Design
text:
Step 3: Link the Model File
Select the model .pkml file that will be used for the scenario. The file must be located in the folder defined in the ProjectConfiguration (Models/Simulations/ by default).

Scenarios





Important


File name only — not the full path.




At this point, the user can already run the simulation with the default “scenario” defined in the pkml file.

title: Scenario Design

Step 4: Model Parameters
Model parameter sets are defined in Model Parameters view. Each sheet is one parameter set to apply.
Multiple sheets can be selected. In this case, the parameters from all sheets will be applied, with later sheets taking precedence in case of conflicts.






Scenarios







Model Parameters

Individual biometrics are defined in the Individual Biometrics tab.
Additional parameters can be set in an individual-specific parameter set whose name matches the individual name.

Protein ontogenies adjust protein expression with age.

Populations are defined in the Populations tab:

text:
Population from CSV
Populations can also come as .csv files (exported from PK-Sim or {ospsuite}).
Place such files in the PopulationsCSV/ folder. Then:

Set PopulationId to the .csv file name (without extension).
Set ReadPopulationFromCSV to TRUE.


Scenarios

Step 7: Time

Scenarios

title: Scenario Design
text:
Step 8: Output Paths
Output Paths define the paths to molecules or parameters whose outputs will be stored for post-processing.
Define them once in the dedicated Output Paths tab — each row gets a unique OutputPathId plus its full OutputPath. Scenarios and other configuration sections then reference the OutputPathId, so the same path can be reused across multiple scenarios without retyping.

text:
Output Paths
Several IDs can be combined per scenario, comma-separated.






Output Paths







Scenarios

title: Scenario Design
text:
Step 9: Administration Protocol

Different study designs are simulated by parameterizing application protocols.
R does not yet support creating new administration protocols.
Must rely on the pre-defined structure in the simulation .pkml.
Switch protocols by setting application parameters: dose, administration time, infusion duration, etc.

Select & Parameterize a Protocol

title: Scenario Design

Parametrization Hierarchy
Parameters are applied top-to-bottom — later layers override earlier ones for the same path.





flowchart TB
  P1["<div style='text-align:center'><b>1. .pkml defaults</b><br/><i>simulation file</i></div>"]
  P2["<div style='text-align:center'><b>2. Individual</b><br/><i>Individuals.xlsx</i></div>"]

  P5["<div style='text-align:center'><b>5. Application protocol</b><br/><i>Applications.xlsx</i></div>"]

title: Scenario Design
text:
Summary








Step
Function
What it does




1
readScenarioConfigurationFromExcel()
Parse Excel into ScenarioConfiguration — settings only, no model loaded.


2
createScenarios()
Build Scenario — loads .pkml, applies parameters/individual/population/time/outputs.


3
runScenarios()
Execute simulations, return results by scenario name.



Configuration vs Scenario — ScenarioConfiguration = declaration. Scenario = realized simulation object with model loaded.

title: Scenario Design
text:
Excel ↔︎ JSON Recap

The R workflow (readScenarioConfigurationFromExcel() and friends) reads from the Excel files in Configurations/.
The ESQapp, however, writes directly into the JSON snapshot (ProjectConfiguration.json).
After editing the project in the ESQapp, the Excel files are out of sync. Restore them before running the R workflow:

restoreProjectConfiguration("ProjectConfiguration.json")






Tip


Use projectConfigurationStatus() to check whether the Excel files and the JSON snapshot are in sync (covered in B2).

title: Scenario Design
text:
Create a ScenarioConfiguration
Create a ScenarioConfiguration containing all the settings defined in the configuration files.

projectConfiguration <- createProjectConfiguration(
  exampleProjectConfigurationPath(),
  ignoreVersionCheck = TRUE
)

# projectConfiguration tells `{esqlabsR}` where to find the configuration files
scenarioConfigurations <- readScenarioConfigurationFromExcel(
  scenarioNames = "TestScenario",
  projectConfiguration = projectConfiguration
)







Tip


ignoreVersionCheck = TRUE is used here because the example project bundled with the package may have been created with a different version of {esqlabsR}. You will not need this in your own projects.

title: Sensitivity Analysis
section: 🎯 Goal: Quantify parameter influence on PK outcomes
text: 🎯 Goal: Quantify parameter influence on PK outcomes

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Why sensitivity analysis?

Quantify how a change in an input parameter shifts a PK outcome (C_max, t_max, AUC, …).
Rank parameters by influence — focus model refinement on what matters.
Detect non-linear behaviour (e.g. saturation).

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Workflow





flowchart LR
  A["readScenarioConfigurationFromExcel()"] --> B["createScenarios()"]
  B --> C["scenarios$X$simulation"]
  C --> D["sensitivityCalculation()"]
  D --> E1["sensitivitySpiderPlot()"]
  D --> E2["sensitivityTornadoPlot()"]
  D --> E3["sensitivityTimeProfiles()"]













{esqlabsR} vs {ospsuite} sensitivity analysis


{ospsuite}::sensitivityAnalysis() performs a local sensitivity analysis: each parameter perturbed once around its nominal value, single sensitivity index per (parameter, PK metric) pair.
{esqlabsR}::sensitivityCalculation() extends this with:

multi-factor scaling sweep (variationRange) → reveals non-linear / saturation behaviour,
custom output functions beyond C_max / t_max / AUC_inf,
ready-made spider / tornado / time-profile plots,
saveSensitivityCalculation() / loadSensitivityCalculation() for expensive runs.

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Sensitivity index
For a parameter p and PK outcome y:
\[
S = \frac{\Delta y / y}{\Delta p / p}
\]

S = 1 → 1 % parameter change ⇒ 1 % PK change.
S = 0 → no impact.
Sign → direction (positive: same direction; negative: inverse).

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Build a scenario
sensitivityCalculation() operates on a Simulation object. In the {esqlabsR} workflow the simulation is created by a scenario — reuse the same path as B3 / B4 instead of loading the .pkml directly.


Code
projectConfigurationPath <- exampleProjectConfigurationPath()

projectConfiguration <- createProjectConfiguration(
  projectConfigurationPath,
  ignoreVersionCheck = TRUE
)

scenarioConfigurations <- readScenarioConfigurationFromExcel(
  scenarioNames = "TestScenario",
  projectConfiguration = projectConfiguration
)

scenarios <- createScenarios(scenarioConfigurations)

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Grab the underlying simulation
Each Scenario exposes its loaded simulation (with parameters, individual, protocol, and time intervals already applied) via $simulation.


Code
simulation <- scenarios$TestScenario$simulation








Tip


List every parameter path of the loaded simulation with ospsuite::getAllParameterPathsIn(simulation).

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Output paths
Simulation output paths for which sensitivity will be assessed. Each PK parameter (or custom output function) is calculated for every output path — one sensitivity result set per (output path, PK parameter) combination.
Named vector — names appear as labels in plots.

outputPaths <- c(
  Plasma = "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)"
)







Tip


Navigate the simulation tree with ospsuite::getSimulationTree(simulation) to find output paths.

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Parameter paths
Example - vary four PBPK-relevant inputs.

parameterPaths <- c(
  Lipophilicity = "Aciclovir|Lipophilicity",
  `Fraction unbound` = "Aciclovir|Fraction unbound (plasma, reference value)",
  Dose = "Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Dose",
  `Tubular secretion` = "Neighborhoods|Kidney_pls_Kidney_ur|Aciclovir|Renal Clearances-TS-Aciclovir|Tubular secretion"
)







Tip


Names of the vector → labels on plots / tables. Pick descriptive ones.









Tip


Find alle parameters that could be used in sensitivity analysis with ospsuite::getAllParameterPathsIn(simulation).

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
sensitivityCalculation()
analysis <- sensitivityCalculation(
  simulation     = simulation,
  outputPaths    = outputPaths,
  parameterPaths = parameterPaths,
  variationRange = c(0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 10),
  pkParameters   = c("C_max", "t_max", "AUC_inf")
)
What it does:

Re-runs the simulation once per (parameter, factor) pair.
Scales each parameter by every value in variationRange.
Computes the requested PK metrics + the sensitivity index.

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Run it


Code
analysis <- sensitivityCalculation(
  simulation = simulation,
  outputPaths = outputPaths,
  parameterPaths = parameterPaths
)

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Inspect analysis$pkData

head(analysis$pkData)


  



Columns: ParameterPath, ParameterFactor, ParameterValue, PKParameter, PKParameterValue, PKPercentChange, SensitivityPKParameter.

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Spider plot
PK metric on y, scaling factor on x — one panel per metric, one line per parameter.

sensitivitySpiderPlot(analysis)

$`Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)`

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Tornado plot
Rank parameters by influence at a fixed scaling — bars compare 0.1× vs 10× by default.

sensitivityTornadoPlot(analysis)

$`Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)`

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Time profiles
Full concentration–time curve per scaling factor (blue → black → red as factor increases).

sensitivityTimeProfiles(analysis)

$`Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)`

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Custom output functions
customOutputFunctions — named list of functions with parameters x (time), y (output values), or both. Each must return a single number.


Code
customOutputFunctions <- list(
  MeanConcentration = function(y) mean(y),
  NormalizedConcentration = function(y) mean(y / max(y))
)








Tip



x — simulation time vector (minutes); y — output vector (base unit of the output)
Pass pkParameters = character(0) to compute only custom metrics.

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Run with custom functions


Code
analysis_custom <- sensitivityCalculation(
  simulation = simulation,
  outputPaths = outputPaths,
  parameterPaths = parameterPaths,
  pkParameters = character(0),
  customOutputFunctions = customOutputFunctions
)

head(analysis_custom$pkData)



  



Custom metrics appear in the PKParameter column — the same structure as standard PK parameters.

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Visualise custom results

sensitivityTornadoPlot(analysis_custom)

$`Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)`

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Save / load
Sensitivity runs are expensive — persist them.
saveSensitivityCalculation(analysis, outputDir = "sensitivity-results")

# Later session
analysis <- loadSensitivityCalculation(
  outputDir  = "sensitivity-results",
  simulation = simulation
)

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Exercise 2: Run a sensitivity calculation
Vary lipophilicity, fraction unbound, dose, tubular secretion — track plasma.

Build the named outputPaths and parameterPaths vectors.
Call sensitivityCalculation() with defaults — assign to analysis.
head(analysis$pkData).



Solution
outputPaths <- c(
  Plasma = "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)"
)

parameterPaths <- c(
  Lipophilicity = "Aciclovir|Lipophilicity",
  `Fraction unbound` = "Aciclovir|Fraction unbound (plasma, reference value)",
  Dose = "Events|IV 250mg 10min|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Dose",
  `Tubular secretion` = "Neighborhoods|Kidney_pls_Kidney_ur|Aciclovir|Renal Clearances-TS-Aciclovir|Tubular secretion"
)

analysis <- sensitivityCalculation(
  simulation = simulation,
  outputPaths = outputPaths,
  parameterPaths = parameterPaths
)

head(analysis$pkData)



  









Tip


Path mismatch errors → confirm with ospsuite::getAllParameterPathsIn(simulation) and ospsuite::getAllStateVariablesPaths(simulation).

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Exercise 3: Spider plot

Generate a spider plot of analysis.
Identify the PK metric most affected by Tubular secretion.

Rank parameters by their influence on AUC_inf.

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Exercise 5: Time profiles + interpretation

Plot the time profiles.
Which parameter has the strongest visual effect on the terminal slope of the plasma curve? Why?



Solution
sensitivityTimeProfiles(analysis)


$`Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)`









Tip


Terminal slope reflects elimination — parameters touching clearance pathways (e.g. tubular secretion) dominate it.

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Exercise 6 (optional): Persist results

Save analysis to sensitivity-results/ with saveSensitivityCalculation().
In a new R session: rebuild the scenario (createScenarios()), pull simulation from it, then reload with loadSensitivityCalculation().
Verify by re-running sensitivitySpiderPlot() on the loaded object.



Solution
saveSensitivityCalculation(
  analysis,
  outputDir = "sensitivity-results",
  overwrite = TRUE
)

# Fresh session — rebuild the scenario, then reload the analysis.
scenarios <- createScenarios(scenarioConfigurations)
simulation <- scenarios$TestScenario$simulation

analysis <- loadSensitivityCalculation(
  outputDir = "sensitivity-results",
  simulation = simulation
)

sensitivitySpiderPlot(analysis)


$`Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)`

title: Sensitivity Analysis
text:
Exercise 7 (optional): Custom output function

Define a custom function MaxEliminationRate returning the steepest (most negative) slope of the concentration–time curve.
Run sensitivityCalculation() with pkParameters = character(0) and only that function.
Generate a tornado plot — does the ranking match Exercise 4?



Solution
customFunctions7 <- list(
  MaxEliminationRate = function(x, y) {
    slopes <- diff(y) / diff(x)
    abs(min(slopes))
  }
)

analysis7 <- sensitivityCalculation(
  simulation = simulation,
  outputPaths = outputPaths,
  parameterPaths = parameterPaths,
  pkParameters = character(0),
  customOutputFunctions = customFunctions7
)

sensitivityTornadoPlot(analysis7)


$`Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Aciclovir|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)`
